topics.title: Familiarisation with basic Linux programming commands: ps, strace, strings, objdump, file, time, fuser, top
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL407 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarisation with basic Linux programming commands: ps, strace, strings, objdump, file, time, fuser, top

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Linux/Unix Philosophy of Command-Line Utilities

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems Lab**, this experiment introduces students to the foundational **system introspection** and **binary analysis** utilities that are essential for understanding how a Linux system operates *under the hood*. These eight commands collectively allow a programmer to:

1. **Inspect** running processes (`ps`, `top`, `fuser`).
2. **Trace** the interaction between a program and the Linux kernel (`strace`).
3. **Analyze** compiled binaries at the object/machine-code level (`objdump`, `strings`).
4. **Profile** and **measure** execution time (`time`).
5. **Identify** the nature of a file without opening it (`file`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL407 - Module 2):** The official KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Operating Systems Lab explicitly requires students to "familiarise with basic Linux programming commands used for process inspection, system call tracing, and binary file analysis." This experiment is a **direct mapping** to that learning outcome.

## 1.2 Conceptual Definition of Each Command

| # | Command | Formal Definition | Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | **`ps`** | **P**rocess **S**tatus. Reports a snapshot of currently active processes in the process table. | Like taking a **photograph** of everyone currently in a busy office — it shows *who* is running, *how long* they have been there, and *what* they are doing. |
| 2 | **`strace`** | A diagnostic/debugging tool that intercepts and records the **system calls** made by a process and the signals it receives. | Like attaching a **black box flight recorder** to a program. Every time the program "knocks on the kernel's door" (system call), strace notes it down. |
| 3 | **`strings`** | Extracts and prints the sequences of printable characters (length $\geq$ **4 bytes** by default) found within a binary or object file. | Like running a **metal detector** over a compiled program to find the readable text messages (like error messages) hidden inside the binary. |
| 4 | **`objdump`** | A GNU binary utility that displays information from one or more **object files**, including disassembly of machine code, headers, and symbol tables. | Like an **X-ray machine** that lets you see the internal anatomy of a compiled `.o` or executable file. |
| 5 | **`file`** | Performs a series of tests (magic number inspection, language analysis) to classify a file's type. | Like a **fingerprint scanner** — even if a file has no extension or a misleading one, `file` reveals what it really *is*. |
| 6 | **`time`** | Runs a given command and reports its **elapsed wall-clock time**, **CPU user time**, and **CPU system time** after completion. | Like a **stopwatch** at a sports event — it doesn't just measure the total time, but splits it into "the runner's effort" (user) and "the wind/ground assistance" (system). |
| 7 | **`fuser`** | Identifies which **PIDs** are currently using a specified file, directory, or socket. | Like a **parking attendant** who tells you *which cars* are currently parked in a particular spot, so you know who to ask to move. |
| 8 | **`top`** | Provides a dynamic, **real-time**, continuously-updated view of running system processes and resource usage (CPU, memory). | Like a **car dashboard** showing live speed, RPM, and fuel — it refreshes every few seconds so you always see the current state. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Format Convention:** Linux reports time in three categories: `real` (wall clock), `user` (CPU spent in user-mode code), and `sys` (CPU spent in kernel-mode code). The fundamental relationship is:
> $$\text{real} \approx \text{user} + \text{sys} + \text{I/O wait} + \text{scheduling latency}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet

## 2.1 Command-by-Command Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 `ps` — Process Status

**Theoretical Foundation:** Every process in Linux is represented by a `task_struct` inside kernel memory. The `/proc` pseudo-filesystem exposes this as a numbered directory for each PID. The `ps` command reads from `/proc` and formats the data into columns.

**Core columns produced by `ps -ef`:**

| Column | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **UID** | User ID of the process owner |
| **PID** | Unique Process Identifier (kernel-allocated integer) |
| **PPID** | Parent Process ID (the process that forked this one) |
| **C / %CPU** | CPU utilization (legacy integer in `ps`; fractional in `top`) |
| **STIME** | Start time of the process |
| **TTY** | Controlling terminal (`?` means daemon / no terminal) |
| **TIME** | Cumulative CPU time consumed |
| **CMD** | The exact command/arguments that started the process |

**The `STAT` field (from `ps aux`):**

| Letter | State | Description |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| `R` | Running | Currently executing or in the run queue |
| `S` | Sleeping | Interruptible sleep (waiting for an event) |
| `D` | Uninterruptible | Usually doing disk I/O; cannot be killed by signals |
| `Z` | Zombie | Finished execution, awaiting parent to reap its exit status |
| `T` | Stopped | Suspended (e.g., via SIGSTOP) or being traced |
| `I` | Idle | Kernel idle thread |

### 2.1.2 `strace` — System Call Tracer

**Theoretical Foundation:** Linux provides the `ptrace()` system call, which allows one process to observe and control another. `strace` is a userspace wrapper around `ptrace`. When you run `strace ./program`, the kernel **stops the child at every system call boundary**, hands control to `strace`, which then logs the call and resumes the child.

**What gets logged:** Every system call name (e.g., `open`, `read`, `write`, `mmap`, `clone`, `exit`), its arguments, and the return value. Signals received are also printed.

### 2.1.3 `strings` — Printable String Extractor

**Theoretical Foundation:** Compiled binaries contain literal character arrays (string constants). Even after compilation, these raw bytes remain in the `.rodata` or `.data` section of the ELF file. `strings` scans for runs of printable ASCII/Unicode characters of a configurable minimum length (default: **4 characters**).

**Engineering Utility:** Reverse engineering, malware analysis, finding hardcoded credentials (a real-world security audit technique), and verifying embedded version strings.

### 2.1.4 `objdump` — Object File Dumper

**Theoretical Foundation:** The **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** is the standard binary format on Linux. `objdump` parses the ELF sections (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.symtab`, `.strtab`) and can **disassemble** the `.text` section from raw machine bytes back into human-readable assembly (e.g., x86-64 `AT&T` or `Intel` syntax).

**Key sub-options:**

| Option | Function |
|--------|----------|
| `-d` | Disassemble executable sections |
| `-D` | Disassemble **all** sections (more verbose) |
| `-h` | Show section headers |
| `-t` | Show the symbol table |
| `-f` | Show the file header (architecture, entry point) |
| `-x` | Show **all** available headers (most verbose) |
| `-M intel` | Use Intel syntax instead of AT&T |

### 2.1.5 `file` — File Type Identifier

**Theoretical Foundation:** `file` reads the **magic number** (a unique byte signature at a fixed offset) and compares it against the compiled `magic` database (`/usr/share/file/magic.mgc`). Examples of magic numbers:

| File Type | Magic Bytes (hex) | Offset |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| ELF executable | `7F 45 4C 46` (`\x7FELF`) | 0 |
| PNG image | `89 50 4E 47 0D 0A 1A 0A` | 0 |
| PDF document | `25 50 44 46 2D` (`%PDF-`) | 0 |
| gzip archive | `1F 8B` | 0 |
| C source (heuristic) | text patterns | varies |

### 2.1.6 `time` — Command Timer

**Theoretical Foundation:** Linux tracks time using two clocks: `CLOCK_MONOTONIC` (steady, not affected by system time changes) for `real`, and process-specific CPU timers (accumulated in `task_struct->utime` and `task_struct->stime`) for `user` and `sys`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **There are TWO `time` commands:**
> 1. The **Bash builtin** (`type time`) — limited output.
> 2. The **GNU `/usr/bin/time`** standalone utility — verbose output via `-v` flag. KTU exams typically expect the GNU utility's behaviour.

### 2.1.7 `fuser` — File User Identifier

**Theoretical Foundation:** `fuser` walks the kernel's open-file table via `/proc/<pid>/fd` and reports which PIDs hold an open file descriptor to the target. It can also **send a signal** to those PIDs (e.g., `fuser -k` to kill them). This is the recommended way to safely unmount a busy filesystem.

### 2.1.8 `top` — Task Overview Process

**Theoretical Foundation:** `top` reads `/proc/stat`, `/proc/meminfo`, and `/proc/[pid]/stat` at a configurable interval (default **3 seconds**). It computes CPU usage as a **decaying average** to give a smooth, real-time visualisation.

**Critical interactive shortcuts (for the lab):**

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `P` | Sort by CPU usage |
| `M` | Sort by memory usage |
| `k` | Kill a process (prompts for PID) |
| `r` | Renice a process (change priority) |
| `1` | Toggle per-CPU core breakdown |
| `q` | Quit |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Options Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **most-asked reference** in KTU viva-voce and Part-A questions for this experiment. Memorise it.

| Command | Most Useful Option Combination | What It Does |
|---------|-------------------------------|--------------|
| `ps` | `ps -ef` | Full-format listing of **all** processes |
| `ps` | `ps aux` | BSD-style listing with `%CPU` and `%MEM` |
| `strace` | `strace -o out.log ./prog` | Trace and save to `out.log` |
| `strace` | `strace -e trace=open,read ./prog` | Trace **only** specific syscalls |
| `strace` | `strace -c ./prog` | Print a **summary table** of syscalls |
| `strings` | `strings -n 8 binary` | Minimum string length = 8 |
| `strings` | `strings -a binary` | Scan the **whole** file (not just loaded sections) |
| `objdump` | `objdump -d binary` | Disassemble executable code |
| `objdump` | `objdump -t binary` | Show symbol table |
| `objdump` | `objdump -h binary` | Show section headers |
| `file` | `file *` | Identify **all** files in current directory |
| `time` | `/usr/bin/time -v ./prog` | Verbose timing report |
| `fuser` | `fuser -v /mnt/data` | Show verbose info about users of `/mnt/data` |
| `fuser` | `fuser -k /mnt/data` | **Kill** processes using `/mnt/data` |
| `top` | `top -u username` | Show only processes of a specific user |
| `top` | `top -p 1234` | Monitor only PID 1234 |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These commands form the **first-day toolkit** of every Linux systems programmer, DevOps engineer, and security analyst. The 2024 KTU syllabus places this experiment as the *gateway* to advanced labs (process scheduling, IPC) that follow in Modules 3, 4, and 5.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Execution Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Category → Command → Purpose → Typical KTU Viva Question

| Category | Command | Purpose | Sample Viva Question |
|----------|---------|---------|----------------------|
| Process Inspection | `ps`, `top` | See running processes | *"How do you find the PID of your shell?"* |
| System Call Tracing | `strace` | See kernel interactions | *"Why does `ls` make an `openat` call?"* |
| Binary Analysis | `objdump`, `strings` | Examine compiled code | *"How do you confirm a binary is 64-bit?"* |
| File Identification | `file` | Detect true file type | *"Why is `file` more reliable than the extension?"* |
| Performance Timing | `time` | Measure execution cost | *"What is the difference between `real` and `user` time?"* |
| Resource Lock Detection | `fuser` | Find who is using a file | *"Why does `umount` fail with 'device busy'?"* |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Practical Execution

### Experiment Step 1 — Process Inspection with `ps`

**Step 1.1:** Open a terminal in your KTU lab VM and run:
```bash
ps
```
**Expected output:** A list of processes attached to the **current terminal** only. By default, `ps` (without options) shows only the processes of the **current session**.

**Step 1.2:** Now run the full listing:
```bash
ps -ef
```
**Expected output:** Every running process on the system, with columns `UID, PID, PPID, C, STIME, TTY, TIME, CMD`. Note the `PPID` of your shell — it is usually `1` (for login shells) or the terminal emulator's PID.

**Step 1.3:** Filter for a specific process using `grep` (this is the **standard KTU exam trick**):
```bash
ps -ef | grep sshd
```
**Analysis:** This pipelines the output of `ps` into `grep`, which is a classic **Unix philosophy demonstration** — small tools composed via pipes. The vertical pipe `|` here is a **shell operator**, not a table delimiter.

**Step 1.4:** Identify your shell's PID:
```bash
echo $$
```
**Analytical Note:** The shell variable `$$` expands to the PID of the *current* shell process. This is the **fastest** way to find your own PID, and it is a common KTU short-answer question.

### Experiment Step 2 — Dynamic Monitoring with `top`

**Step 2.1:** Launch `top`:
```bash
top
```
**Step 2.2:** Observe the header. The key values are:
- `load average: 0.05, 0.10, 0.15` — the three values represent the **1-minute, 5-minute, and 15-minute** exponentially decayed load averages.
- `%Cpu(s): 1.2 us, 0.5 sy, 0.0 ni, 98.0 id, 0.3 wa, 0.0 hi, 0.0 si, 0.0 st` — broken down into `us` (user), `sy` (system/kernel), `id` (idle), `wa` (I/O wait).

**Step 2.3:** Press `1` to expand the CPU line into per-core statistics. Press `P` to sort by CPU, `M` to sort by memory. Press `q` to quit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Average Interpretation Rule (commonly asked in viva):** A load average of `1.0` on a single-core system means **100% utilisation**. On an $n$-core system, a load average of $n$ means full utilisation. Therefore the **normalised load** is:
> $$\text{Normalised Load} = \frac{\text{Load Average}}{n_{\text{cores}}}$$

### Experiment Step 3 — System Call Tracing with `strace`

**Step 3.1:** Create a tiny C program to trace:
```bash
cat > hello.c << 'EOF'
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    printf("Hello KTU\n");
    return 0;
}
EOF
gcc hello.c -o hello
```

**Step 3.2:** Trace it:
```bash
strace ./hello
```
**Expected output:** A long list of system calls. The final line should be `write(1, "Hello KTU\n", 10) = 10`, followed by `exit_group(0) = ?`. This proves that `printf` ultimately invokes the `write()` syscall to file descriptor **1** (stdout).

**Step 3.3:** Save and summarise:
```bash
strace -c ./hello
```
**Analysis:** The `-c` flag produces a **summary table** showing each syscall, its count, total time, and error count — invaluable for performance analysis.

**Step 3.4:** Trace a *specific* syscall only:
```bash
strace -e trace=openat ./hello
```
**Analysis:** This filters the trace to only `openat` calls, which is what `gcc`-compiled programs use to open shared libraries (e.g., `libc.so.6`).

### Experiment Step 4 — Binary Analysis with `file`, `strings`, `objdump`

**Step 4.1:** Identify the file type:
```bash
file hello
```
**Expected output:** `hello: ELF 64-bit LSB pie executable, x86-64, ... dynamically linked, ...`

**Analytical Note:** The string `ELF 64-bit LSB pie executable` tells us: **(a)** it's an ELF binary, **(b)** it's 64-bit, **(c)** it's **LSB** (Least Significant Byte first, i.e., little-endian), and **(d)** it's **PIE** (Position Independent Executable — a security feature).

**Step 4.2:** Extract printable strings:
```bash
strings hello
```
**Expected output:** You will see `Hello KTU`, plus many library paths like `/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6`.

**Step 4.3:** Disassemble the `main` function:
```bash
objdump -d hello | grep -A 20 '<main>'
```
**Expected output:** x86-64 assembly instructions. The `call` instruction targeting `printf@plt` confirms where the formatted output is dispatched.

**Step 4.4:** Inspect ELF sections:
```bash
objdump -h hello
```
**Expected output:** A table of sections like `.text` (code), `.data` (initialised globals), `.rodata` (read-only data — where the string literal lives), `.bss` (uninitialised globals).

### Experiment Step 5 — Performance Timing with `time`

**Step 5.1:** Time a CPU-bound task:
```bash
time find / -name "*.c" > /dev/null
```
**Expected output:**
```
real    0m2.341s
user    0m0.812s
sys     0m1.205s
```

**Analytical interpretation:**
- `real` = wall-clock time (2.341 s).
- `user` = CPU time spent in user mode (0.812 s).
- `sys` = CPU time spent in kernel mode (1.205 s).
- Notice that `user + sys < real`. The difference (0.324 s) is spent in **I/O wait** and **scheduling**.

**Mathematical relationship:**
$$\text{real} \geq \text{user} + \text{sys}$$
The equality holds only when the process is **CPU-bound and single-threaded** and never blocks on I/O. In multi-core scenarios, `user + sys` can *exceed* `real` because the OS can use multiple cores in parallel.

**Step 5.2:** Verbose timing with the GNU utility:
```bash
/usr/bin/time -v find / -name "*.c" > /dev/null
```
**Expected output:** Includes `Maximum resident set size (kbytes)`, `Voluntary context switches`, `Involuntary context switches`, etc. — extremely useful for KTU viva questions on **process behaviour**.

### Experiment Step 6 — File Usage Detection with `fuser`

**Step 6.1:** Try to unmount a USB drive and see the error:
```bash
umount /mnt/usb
# umount: /mnt/usb: device is busy.
```

**Step 6.2:** Find out *who* is using it:
```bash
fuser -v /mnt/usb
```
**Expected output:** A table with columns `USER`, `PID`, `ACCESS`, and `COMMAND`. `ACCESS` shows the file mode (e.g., `e` for executable, `r` for read, `w` for write, `f` for open file).

**Step 6.3:** Kill the offending process:
```bash
fuser -k /mnt/usb
```
**Step 6.4:** Now you can unmount safely:
```bash
umount /mnt/usb
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The question *"A system administrator cannot unmount a filesystem. How would you diagnose and resolve this using a single command family?"* has `fuser` as its textbook answer.

## 3.3 Decision Tree: "Which Command Should I Use?"

```text
+-- I want to SEE running processes
|     +-- One-time snapshot -> ps
|     +-- Continuously updated -> top
|
+-- I want to know WHO is using a file
|     +-- fuser
|
+-- I want to know WHAT a binary is
|     +-- file   (what type?)
|     +-- strings (what readable text is inside?)
|     +-- objdump (what does the machine code look like?)
|
+-- I want to know HOW LONG a command took
|     +-- time
|
+-- I want to know WHAT THE PROGRAM IS DOING
      +-- strace  (what kernel calls does it make?)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How These Commands Interact with the Linux Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userShell["User Shell Session"] --> execCmd["Execute Command"]
    execCmd --> disp1{"Command Type"}
    disp1 -->|"ps / top / fuser"| procTab["Read /proc Filesystem"]
    proc1["Process Table in Kernel Memory"] --> procTab
    procTab --> out1["Formatted Output to Terminal"]

    disp1 -->|"strace"| ptrace["ptrace System Call Hook"]
    execCmd2["Target Program"] --> ptrace
    kernel["Linux Kernel Syscall Boundary"] --> ptrace
    ptrace --> logFile["strace Log File or stdout"]

    disp1 -->|"file / strings / objdump"| elfParse["ELF / Binary Parser"]
    binFile["Compiled Binary on Disk"] --> elfParse
    elfParse --> out2["Analysis Report to Terminal"]

    disp1 -->|"time"| clk["Read CLOCK_MONOTONIC and Process Timers"]
    clk --> out3["real, user, sys Report"]

    style userShell fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style proc1 fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728
    style kernel fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728
    style binFile fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style out1 fill:#E5F5E5,stroke:#2CA02C
    style out2 fill:#E5F5E5,stroke:#2CA02C
    style out3 fill:#E5F5E5,stroke:#2CA02C
    style logFile fill:#E5F5E5,stroke:#2CA02C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue nodes** = user-space entry points.
- **Red nodes** = privileged kernel-space data sources.
- **Orange node** = persistent storage (binary files on disk).
- **Green nodes** = terminal-visible outputs.

## 4.2 Process Lifecycle State Diagram (Relevant to `ps STAT` and `top`)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> R : fork() / exec()
    R --> S : await I/O or event
    S --> R : event ready, scheduled
    R --> D : enter uninterruptible disk wait
    D --> R : I/O completes
    R --> T : SIGSTOP received
    T --> R : SIGCONT received
    R --> Z : exit() but not yet reaped
    Z --> [*] : parent calls wait() / waitpid()
    R --> [*] : exit() and reaped immediately
```

**Reading the diagram:** This state machine is the conceptual model that the `STAT` field in `ps` and the status column in `top` report. Understanding it is **mandatory** for the KTU OS lab viva.

## 4.3 Command Interaction Flow: `time` + `strace` Combined Usage

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Shell
    participant T as time Utility
    participant S as strace Process
    participant K as Linux Kernel
    participant P as Target Program

    U->>T: time strace -c ./hello
    T->>S: fork + exec with start_time
    Note over S: Start monotonic clock
    S->>P: fork + ptrace attach
    P->>K: write(1, "Hello KTU\n", 10)
    K-->>S: syscall intercepted
    S->>S: log to summary table
    K-->>P: resume
    P->>K: exit_group(0)
    P-->>S: process terminated
    Note over S: Compute cumulative user+sys CPU time
    S-->>T: child finished
    Note over T: Stop monotonic clock
    T-->>U: print real, user, sys
```

## 4.4 Mapping Table: KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (CO) → Commands

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO1["CO1: Understand process management concepts"] --> psNode["ps command"]
    CO1 --> topNode["top command"]
    CO1 --> fuserNode["fuser command"]
    CO2["CO2: Analyse system calls and kernel interaction"] --> straceNode["strace command"]
    CO2 --> timeNode["time command"]
    CO3["CO3: Perform binary file analysis"] --> fileNode["file command"]
    CO3 --> stringsNode["strings command"]
    CO3 --> objdumpNode["objdump command"]
    style CO1 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6
    style CO2 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B
    style CO3 fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#1E8449
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*List any three commonly used options of the `ps` command and explain the difference between `ps -ef` and `ps aux`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `ps -ef` uses the **System V** syntax and displays all processes in full format with columns `UID, PID, PPID, C, STIME, TTY, TIME, CMD`. (1 mark)
- `ps aux` uses the **BSD** syntax and includes the additional `%CPU` and `%MEM` columns. The `a` flag means "all users", `u` means "user-oriented format", and `x` includes processes with no controlling terminal. (1 mark)
- **Key difference:** `ps -ef` shows parent PID (`PPID`) and start time (`STIME`); `ps aux` shows percentage CPU and memory. Both are widely used; for a quick view of resource hogs, `ps aux --sort=-%cpu \vert head` is the standard KTU-recommended pattern. (1 mark)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*What is the difference between the shell builtin `time` and the standalone `/usr/bin/time` command? Give one advantage of the GNU utility.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **shell builtin** is a Bash internal command with limited output: only `real, user, sys`. (1 mark)
- The **standalone `/usr/bin/time`** is a separate executable. When invoked directly (with full path), it provides the same three values **plus** additional metrics like maximum resident set size, voluntary context switches, and page faults when used with the `-v` flag. (1 mark)
- **Advantage:** The GNU utility's `-v` (verbose) option gives detailed resource-usage statistics required for performance analysis in OS labs, making it more useful for benchmarking. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the working of the `strace` command. How does it trace system calls made by a program? List any four commonly traced system calls and their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student writes a C program that opens a file and prints its contents. The program crashes with "Segmentation fault" but the student cannot identify the failing line. Describe, step by step, how you would use `strace`, `objdump`, and `strings` together to diagnose this fault. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**Working of `strace` (4 marks):**
`strace` works by using the Linux `ptrace()` system call. The steps are:

1. When the user runs `strace ./program`, the `strace` process becomes the **parent** and `program` becomes the **child**. `strace` calls `ptrace(PTRACE_TRACEME)` indirectly via the child, which causes the kernel to suspend the child at the next system call entry. **(1 mark)**
2. At every system call boundary (entry and exit), the kernel generates a `SIGTRAP` signal that pauses the child and notifies `strace`. **(1 mark)**
3. `strace` reads the child's registers (which hold the syscall number and arguments) and prints them in human-readable form. **(1 mark)**
4. `strace` then resumes the child by calling `ptrace(PTRACE_SYSCALL, ...)`, and the cycle repeats until the child exits. **(1 mark)**

**Four commonly traced system calls (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| Syscall | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `openat(dirfd, pathname, flags, mode)` | Opens a file, returns a file descriptor. Modern Linux uses this in place of the older `open`. |
| `read(fd, buf, count)` | Reads up to `count` bytes from file descriptor `fd` into buffer `buf`. |
| `write(fd, buf, count)` | Writes up to `count` bytes from buffer `buf` to file descriptor `fd`. |
| `mmap(addr, length, prot, flags, fd, offset)` | Maps `length` bytes of the file into the process's virtual address space. |
| `brk(addr)` | Changes the data segment break (used by `malloc` internally). |

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step-by-step diagnostic procedure:**

1. **Compile with debug info** so `objdump` can show line numbers:
   ```bash
   gcc -g -O0 program.c -o program
   ```
   The `-g` flag embeds DWARF debug information into the ELF file. **(1 mark)**

2. **Use `strings` to confirm the source filename and find the strings** the program expects:
   ```bash
   strings program | grep -i "data.txt"
   ```
   This confirms whether the program is trying to open the expected file. **(1 mark)**

3. **Run `strace` immediately before the crash to capture the last few system calls**:
   ```bash
   strace -f -o trace.log ./program
   tail -30 trace.log
   ```
   The `-f` flag follows forked children. The last entry before `SIGSEGV` (Segmentation Fault) will reveal which memory address was accessed and the offset within the file (if the fault was during `read`/`mmap`). **(2 marks)**

4. **Disassemble the `main` function with `objdump`** to map addresses to assembly:
   ```bash
   objdump -d -M intel program | grep -A 30 '<main>:'
   ```
   Locate the `mov`, `call`, or `push` instruction whose operand matches the address reported by `strace`. **(2 marks)**

5. **Cross-reference** the failing address with the line in the source code (using the DWARF info from step 1) to identify the exact C statement causing the segfault. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the `-g` flag while compiling. Without debug info, `objdump` will not show line numbers and the examiner will deduct 2 marks. Always include `-g` in the lab record.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of the `top` command's output. What do the `%Cpu(s)` fields `us, sy, ni, id, wa, hi, si, st` represent? Describe the meaning of the three values in `load average`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the `fuser` command is used to identify and terminate processes that are preventing a filesystem from being unmounted. Also explain the difference between `fuser` and `lsof`. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**Structure of `top` output (3 marks):**

The `top` output has two main regions:

1. **Header summary (5 lines):**
   - Line 1: Current time, system uptime, number of logged-in users, load average.
   - Line 2: Total tasks — `total, running, sleeping, stopped, zombie`.
   - Line 3: CPU usage breakdown (`%Cpu(s)`).
   - Line 4: Memory usage (physical RAM — `total, free, used, buff/cache`).
   - Line 5: Swap usage (virtual memory on disk).

2. **Process table (scrollable):** Columns `PID, USER, PR, NI, VIRT, RES, SHR, S, %CPU, %MEM, TIME+, COMMAND`.

**Meaning of `%Cpu(s)` fields (3 marks — ~0.4 each):**

| Field | Full Name | Meaning |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| `us` | User | CPU time spent running userspace processes. |
| `sy` | System | CPU time spent in the kernel. |
| `ni` | Nice | CPU time spent on niced (low-priority) processes. |
| `id` | Idle | CPU time spent doing nothing. |
| `wa` | I/O wait | CPU time spent waiting for disk I/O. |
| `hi` | Hardware interrupts | Time servicing hardware interrupts. |
| `si` | Software interrupts | Time servicing software (deferred) interrupts. |
| `st` | Steal time | Time the hypervisor "stole" from this VM for other guests. |

**Load average (1 mark):**
The three numbers represent the **exponentially weighted moving average** of the run-queue length over the **last 1, 5, and 15 minutes** respectively. A load average of `1.0` on a single-core machine means 100% busy; on a 4-core machine, 4.0 means fully loaded.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**`fuser` example walkthrough (5 marks):**

```bash
# 1. Try to unmount and observe the error
$ umount /mnt/data
umount: /mnt/data: target is busy.

# 2. Use fuser -v to identify the offender
$ fuser -v /mnt/data
                     USER        PID ACCESS COMMAND
/mnt/data:           root       1234 ..c..  bash
                     alice      5678 F..m.  vim
# ACCESS legend: c=current dir, F=open file, m=memory-mapped

# 3. Kill the processes
$ fuser -k /mnt/data
# By default, fuser -k sends SIGKILL. Use -k -TERM for a polite shutdown.

# 4. Retry unmount
$ umount /mnt/data    # now succeeds
```

**Difference between `fuser` and `lsof` (2 marks):**

| Aspect | `fuser` | `lsof` (List Open Files) |
|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| Output | Only **PIDs** using a given file | **All** open files of a given PID, OR all PIDs using a file |
| Granularity | Coarse — answers "who is using file X?" | Fine-grained — answers "what is PID Y using?" |
| Filtering | By file path only | By PID, user, file, network port, etc. |
| Use case | Quick "device is busy" diagnosis | Forensic / deep inspection |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU OS Lab — Module Experiment 2**

- **`ps`** gives a one-shot snapshot; **`top`** gives a continuous, real-time view. (1 mark question every year)
- The `STAT` column in `ps` uses `R, S, D, Z, T, I` — a **Zombie (`Z`)** process has finished but is waiting for its parent to call `wait()`.
- **`strace`** uses the `ptrace()` system call; it intercepts every transition between user-space and kernel-space.
- The **`-c`** flag in `strace` gives a summary count of syscalls — use it for performance analysis.
- **`strings`** default minimum length is **4 characters**; override with `-n`.
- **`objdump -d`** disassembles executable code; **`-M intel`** switches to Intel syntax (easier for KTU exams than AT&T).
- **`file`** checks the **magic number** at offset 0, not the file extension. The ELF magic is `\x7FELF` (bytes `7F 45 4C 46`).
- **`time`** reports `real, user, sys`. Remember: `real ≥ user + sys`, with equality only for single-threaded CPU-bound tasks.
- **`fuser -v`** shows PID, USER, ACCESS, and COMMAND; `fuser -k` sends SIGKILL by default.
- **`top` load average** has three values: 1-min, 5-min, 15-min. Normalise by dividing by the number of cores.
- Always compile C programs with `gcc -g` to embed debug info before using `objdump` for symbol resolution.
- The **`/proc`** pseudo-filesystem is the data source for `ps`, `top`, and indirectly `fuser`. Knowing this is the conceptual link to the *process management* chapter of OS theory.
- `ps -ef` and `ps aux` are *not* the same — know the difference (System V vs. BSD syntax).
- The `STAT` field's `+` suffix means the process is in the **foreground process group** of its controlling terminal; `<` means **high priority** (not nice); `N` means **low priority** (niced).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
