> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use /proc file system to gather basic information about your machine: (a) Number of CPU cores, (b) Total memory and the fraction of free memory, (c) Number of processes currently running, (d) Number of processes in the running and blocked states, (e) Number of forked since the last bootup, (f) The number of context switches performed since the last bootup for a particular process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The /proc File System — Formal Definition

The **/proc file system** (officially called **procfs**) is a **virtual (pseudo) file system** in Unix and Linux operating systems that resides in memory (RAM) rather than on a physical disk. It provides an **interface to the kernel data structures**, allowing user-space programs and administrators to read (and in some cases, write) live information about running processes, hardware, memory, and kernel parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> The /proc file system is a pseudo file system generated dynamically by the kernel. It does not exist on any physical storage; instead, it is created and maintained in RAM by the kernel to expose internal system information in a file-like format. Each file in /proc corresponds to a kernel variable, a process descriptor, or a hardware register.

The /proc directory is conventionally mounted at the path **/proc** during system boot. It is classified into two main categories:

| Category | Mount Point | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Process-specific subdirectories** | `/proc/<PID>/` | One directory per running process, named by its Process ID (PID) |
| **System-wide information files** | `/proc/cpuinfo`, `/proc/meminfo`, `/proc/stat`, etc. | Global kernel and hardware status |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** Unlike /etc or /home, contents of /proc are **not stored on disk**. They are reconstructed every time you list or read them, reflecting the **live state of the kernel** at that exact instant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car's Dashboard

Imagine your Linux machine is a **car's engine** sealed inside a metal hood. You cannot open the hood and touch the engine, but you have a **dashboard** in front of you with gauges and indicators:

- **Speedometer** → tells you how fast the engine is running (CPU load)
- **Fuel gauge** → tells you how much memory is free (`MemFree`)
- **Odometer** → tells you how many kilometres the car has travelled (context switches since boot)
- **Engine warning lights** → tell you the state of sub-systems (running/blocked processes)

The **/proc file system is that dashboard**. Each "file" inside /proc is a gauge that, when read, asks the kernel: *"What is the current value of this variable?"* and the kernel responds instantly. Just as a dashboard disappears when you turn off the car, /proc disappears when the system is shut down.

## 1.3 Why /proc Matters in Operating System Design

From an **Operating Systems perspective**, /proc satisfies a critical design principle: **separation of policy and mechanism**. The kernel "mechanism" runs in privileged mode and maintains internal data structures. The "policy" of *exposing* that data to user programs is handled by /proc as a clean, file-oriented API. This is why tools like `top`, `ps`, `free`, and `vmstat` work — they all read from /proc behind the scenes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **PID** = Process Identifier (integer, typically 1 to 32768 on 32-bit, up to 4194304 on 64-bit)
> - **Page size** = **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** on x86 Linux — required for memory calculations
> - **Clock tick (USER_HZ)** = **100 Hz** on most Linux systems — used for time accounting
> - **Context switch overhead** ≈ **a few microseconds** on modern CPUs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of /proc — How It Works

The /proc file system is implemented as a **special file system type** registered with the Linux Virtual File System (VFS) layer. When a user-space program calls `open("/proc/meminfo")`, the VFS dispatches the call to the procfs driver's `proc_meminfo_open()` function, which dynamically generates the file's contents from kernel global variables.

**Operational Flow (Step-by-step):**

1. **User process** executes `cat /proc/meminfo` (a system call `read()` reaches the VFS).
2. **VFS layer** identifies the inode as belonging to procfs.
3. **procfs driver** intercepts the read operation and runs its `read_proc` callback.
4. **Callback function** (e.g., `meminfo_proc_show()`) calls `sprintf()` to format kernel global variables (`totalram_pages`, `freeram_pages`) into a text buffer.
5. **Buffer is copied** back to user space via `copy_to_user()`.
6. **No data is cached on disk** — the file is regenerated on every read.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the size of /proc files reported by `ls -l` is often **0 bytes**, even though reading them returns a lot of text. The "size" is the size of the directory entry, not the dynamic content.

## 2.2 The Six Required Information Items — Mapped to /proc Files

The experiment explicitly asks for **six pieces of information**. Each maps to specific files:

| # | Required Information | Primary /proc File(s) | Key Field / Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) | **Number of CPU cores** | `/proc/cpuinfo` | Count of `processor` lines |
| (b) | **Total memory & fraction free** | `/proc/meminfo` | `MemTotal`, `MemFree`, `MemAvailable` |
| (c) | **Number of processes currently running** | `/proc/loadavg` or `/proc/stat` | `procs_running` field |
| (d) | **Processes in running & blocked states** | `/proc/stat` (global) or `/proc/[pid]/stat` (per-process) | `procs_running`, `procs_blocked` |
| (e) | **Number of forks since boot** | `/proc/stat` | `processes` field (cumulative counter) |
| (f) | **Context switches for a particular process** | `/proc/[pid]/status` | `voluntary_ctxt_switches`, `nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches` |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following formulas are **must-know** for any derivation or numerical question on this experiment:

$$
\text{Memory Fraction Free} = \frac{\text{MemFree (in kB)}}{\text{MemTotal (in kB)}}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Used (kB)} = \text{MemTotal} - \text{MemFree} - \text{Buffers} - \text{Cached}
$$

$$
\text{Number of CPU Cores (Logical)} = \text{count of lines matching } \texttt{\^{}processor\$} \text{ in } /proc/cpuinfo
$$

$$
\text{Number of Physical Cores} = \frac{\text{Logical Cores}}{\text{Threads per Core} \times \text{Sockets}}
$$

$$
\text{Total Forks Since Boot} = \text{value of } \texttt{processes} \text{ field in } /proc/stat
$$

$$
\text{Context Switches for PID } p = \text{voluntary\_ctxt\_switches}(p) + \text{nonvoluntary\_ctxt\_switches}(p)
$$

$$
\text{Threads per Core} = 2 \quad \text{(if Hyper-Threading is enabled, otherwise 1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Common Pitfalls:**
> - The unit for `/proc/meminfo` values is **kB (kibibytes)**, not KB. **1 kB = 1024 bytes**.
> - The `/proc/stat` `processes` field is **monotonically increasing** — it never decreases during uptime. It represents the **total number of forks and clones since kernel boot**, not the current count.
> - The "blocked" state in /proc includes processes in **uninterruptible sleep (D state)** waiting on I/O, **interruptible sleep (S state)**, and stopped (T). Some texts treat only D as "blocked"; KTU generally follows the broader definition.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Performance tuning:** System administrators read /proc/loadavg to decide if more servers are needed.
- **Container resource limits:** Docker and Kubernetes read /proc/meminfo and /proc/cpuinfo to enforce cgroup memory and CPU limits.
- **Embedded systems:** On resource-constrained devices, /proc/sys/vm/* lets engineers tune the virtual memory manager (e.g., `swappiness`, `dirty_ratio`).
- **Debugging:** Developers read `/proc/<pid>/maps` to understand memory layout of a process — analogous to using a debugger's memory map view.
- **System monitoring tools:** `top`, `htop`, `vmstat`, `mpstat`, `free`, `nproc` are all thin wrappers around /proc reads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Commands & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 (a) Number of CPU Cores — Derivation via /proc/cpuinfo

### Step 1: Read the file
The `cpuinfo` file contains one logical CPU descriptor per line block. Each block begins with a line of the form:

```
processor       : 0
vendor_id       : GenuineIntel
cpu cores       : 4
siblings        : 8
model name      : Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-8550U CPU @ 1.80GHz
...
```

### Step 2: Apply the count rule
Each block starts with `processor : N`. The **total number of logical CPUs** equals the **number of such lines**:

$$
N_{\text{logical}} = \text{count}\bigl(\{ \ell \in L : \ell \text{ matches } \texttt{\^{}processor\:\}} \bigr)
$$

where $L$ is the set of all lines in `/proc/cpuinfo`.

### Step 3: Shell command implementation

```bash
grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo
```

**Output on a typical 4-core, 8-thread Intel CPU:**

```
8
```

### Step 4: Derive physical cores
If you also need the **physical core count**, read `cpu cores` and `siblings` from the first block:

$$
N_{\text{physical}} = \frac{N_{\text{logical}}}{\text{siblings}/\text{cpu cores}}
$$

On the example machine with `siblings=8` and `cpu cores=4`:
$$
N_{\text{physical}} = \frac{8}{8/4} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ physical cores}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio `siblings / cpu cores` equals the **threads per core** (2 if Hyper-Threading is enabled, 1 otherwise).

## 3.2 (b) Total Memory and Fraction of Free Memory — Derivation via /proc/meminfo

### Step 1: Read the file
Key fields in `/proc/meminfo`:

```
MemTotal:       16384000 kB
MemFree:         8234500 kB
MemAvailable:   11500000 kB
Buffers:          234500 kB
Cached:          2500000 kB
...
```

### Step 2: Identify the values
- **Total physical RAM** (less reserved kernel bits): $M_{\text{total}} = \text{MemTotal} = 16384000$ kB
- **Currently free**: $M_{\text{free}} = \text{MemFree} = 8234500$ kB
- **Available for new applications** (kernel estimate): $M_{\text{avail}} = \text{MemAvailable} = 11500000$ kB

### Step 3: Compute fraction of free memory

$$
f_{\text{free}} = \frac{M_{\text{free}}}{M_{\text{total}}} = \frac{8234500}{16384000} = 0.5027\ldots
$$

Expressed as a percentage:
$$
f_{\text{free}} \times 100\% \approx 50.27\%
$$

### Step 4: Cross-verification via page count
Linux also exposes the raw page count. Since each page is **4096 bytes** = **4 KiB**:

$$
\text{MemTotal in kB} = \frac{\text{MemTotal in pages} \times 4096}{1024} = \text{MemTotal in pages} \times 4
$$

So `MemTotal in pages` $= 16384000 \div 4 = 4096000$ pages. This matches the value reported in `/proc/zoneinfo` and `/proc/pagetypeinfo` and confirms consistency.

### Step 5: Shell command implementation

```bash
grep -E "MemTotal|MemFree|MemAvailable" /proc/meminfo
awk '/MemTotal/ {total=$2} /MemFree/ {free=$2} END {printf "Fraction Free = %.4f\n", free/total}' /proc/meminfo
```

## 3.3 (c) & (d) Running and Blocked Processes — Derivation via /proc/stat

### Step 1: Read the relevant portion of /proc/stat
The `processes` family of fields in `/proc/stat` (or directly in `/proc/loadavg`) reports the live task counts.

```
$ cat /proc/loadavg
0.45 0.60 0.55 1/234 5678
```

The fields are: `1-min load avg | 5-min load avg | 15-min load avg | running/total tasks | last PID assigned`.

From the example: **1 process currently running, 234 total tasks** (kernel threads + user processes).

### Step 2: More precise reading from /proc/stat
For a system-wide summary including blocked processes:

```bash
$ grep -E "procs_running|procs_blocked" /proc/stat
procs_running 2
procs_blocked 0
```

This means **2 processes are in the R (runnable) state** and **0 are in the D (uninterruptible sleep) state** at the instant the file was read.

### Step 3: Per-process state inspection
For a particular process (say PID 1234):

```bash
$ cat /proc/1234/stat
1234 (bash) S 1233 1234 1234 34816 1235 4194304 ...
```

The **3rd field** (after PID and command name in parentheses) is the **state character**:

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **R** | Running (or runnable, on a run queue) |
| **S** | Interruptible sleep (waiting on event, e.g., I/O completion, signal) |
| **D** | Uninterruptible sleep (typically I/O wait — "blocked" in classic OS terminology) |
| **Z** | Zombie (terminated, parent not yet `wait()`ed) |
| **T** | Stopped (signal SIGSTOP or being traced) |
| **I** | Idle kernel thread |

### Step 4: Aggregate count via shell
To count all processes in each state across the entire system:

```bash
ps -e -o stat= | sort | uniq -c
```

**Sample output:**

```
   1 I
 187 S
   3 R
   2 D
   0 T
   0 Z
```

This gives: **3 running (R), 2 blocked (D)** — directly answering part (d).

## 3.4 (e) Number of Forks Since Boot — Derivation via /proc/stat

### Step 1: Read the `processes` field

```bash
$ grep ^processes /proc/stat
processes 145678
```

### Step 2: Interpret the value
The `processes` field is incremented by the kernel's `do_fork()` function (in `kernel/fork.c`) every time a new task is created — whether by `fork()`, `vfork()`, or `clone()` system calls. It is a **monotonically increasing counter**.

$$
N_{\text{forks}}(t) = \text{value of } \texttt{processes} \text{ at time } t
$$

### Step 3: Compute the fork rate (derived metric)
If you sample twice with a known interval $\Delta t$, the **fork rate** is:

$$
R_{\text{fork}} = \frac{N_{\text{forks}}(t_2) - N_{\text{forks}}(t_1)}{t_2 - t_1} \quad \text{[forks/second]}
$$

This is useful for detecting fork bombs or runaway daemons.

## 3.5 (f) Context Switches for a Particular Process — Derivation via /proc/<pid>/status

### Step 1: Choose a target process
Identify the PID of a process (e.g., the current shell):

```bash
echo $$
# Output: 5678
```

### Step 2: Read its context-switch counters

```bash
$ grep -E "ctxt_switches|^Name|^Pid|^State" /proc/5678/status
Name:   bash
Pid:    5678
State:  S (sleeping)
voluntary_ctxt_switches:        142
nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches:     89
```

### Step 3: Compute the total context switches for the process

$$
C_{\text{total}}(p) = V(p) + N(p)
$$

where $V(p)$ is voluntary and $N(p)$ is non-voluntary. For the example:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(5678) = 142 + 89 = 231 \text{ context switches}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the two types
- **Voluntary** ($V$): the process yielded the CPU itself (e.g., called `sleep()`, `read()` on a pipe with no data, `wait()`).
- **Non-voluntary** ($N$): the kernel preempted the process because its **time slice (quantum) expired** or a higher-priority process became runnable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **high non-voluntary count** indicates the process is CPU-bound and being scheduled out frequently — a sign it may need a higher `nice` value or a CPU affinity adjustment.

### Step 5: System-wide context switches (for comparison)

```bash
$ grep ^ctxt /proc/stat
ctxt 12345678
```

This is the total across **all processes** since boot.

## 3.6 Complete Command Cheat-Sheet (Lab Quick Reference)

```bash
# (a) CPU cores
grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo

# (b) Total memory & fraction free
grep MemTotal /proc/meminfo
grep MemFree  /proc/meminfo
awk '/MemTotal/{t=$2}/MemFree/{f=$2}END{printf "Free fraction = %.4f\n",f/t}' /proc/meminfo

# (c) & (d) Running and blocked processes
grep -E "procs_running|procs_blocked" /proc/stat
ps -e -o stat= | sort | uniq -c

# (e) Forks since boot
grep ^processes /proc/stat

# (f) Context switches for a particular process
PID=$$
grep -E "Name|voluntary_ctxt_switches|nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches" /proc/$PID/status
```

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** On a Linux system, `/proc/meminfo` reports `MemTotal: 8192000 kB` and `MemFree: 2048000 kB`. The `/proc/stat` shows `processes 54321` and `procs_running 3`, `procs_blocked 1`. `/proc/cpuinfo` has 8 lines starting with `processor :`. For PID 1001, `/proc/1001/status` shows `voluntary_ctxt_switches: 250` and `nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches: 75`.

**Solution:**

Number of logical CPU cores:
$$
N_{\text{logical}} = 8
$$

Fraction of free memory:
$$
f_{\text{free}} = \frac{2048000}{8192000} = 0.2500 = 25.00\%
$$

Running and blocked processes:
$$
N_{\text{running}} = 3, \quad N_{\text{blocked}} = 1
$$

Forks since boot:
$$
N_{\text{forks}} = 54321
$$

Total context switches for PID 1001:
$$
C_{\text{total}}(1001) = 250 + 75 = 325
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 /proc File System Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **hierarchical tree** of the most important entries under /proc that this experiment touches. It is a structural map, not a physical drawing.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PROC["proc root"]
    PROC --> CPUINFO["cpuinfo - per CPU block"]
    PROC --> MEMINFO["meminfo - RAM stats"]
    PROC --> STAT["stat - kernel counters"]
    PROC --> LOADAVG["loadavg - load average"]
    PROC --> UPTIME["uptime - seconds since boot"]
    PROC --> CMDLINE["cmdline - kernel boot args"]
    PROC --> SYS["sys - tunable kernel params"]
    PROC --> PIDDIR["PID - one subdir per process"]
    PIDDIR --> PIDSTAT["stat - per process state"]
    PIDDIR --> PIDSTATUS["status - human readable info"]
    PIDDIR --> PIDMAPS["maps - memory regions"]
    PIDDIR --> PIDFD["fd - open file descriptors"]
    CPUINFO --> COREFIELD["processor field x N"]
    MEMINFO --> MEMTOTAL["MemTotal field"]
    MEMINFO --> MEMFREE["MemFree field"]
    MEMINFO --> MEMAVAIL["MemAvailable field"]
    STAT --> PROCSRUN["procs_running field"]
    STAT --> PROCSBLK["procs_blocked field"]
    STAT --> PROCESSES["processes - fork counter"]
    STAT --> CTXT["ctxt - global ctxt sw counter"]
    PIDSTATUS --> VOLCTX["voluntary_ctxt_switches"]
    PIDSTATUS --> NVOLCTX["nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches"]
    PIDSTATUS --> STATEFLD["State field R S D Z T"]
```

## 4.2 Information-Extraction Flow (How a User Reads /proc)

The next Mermaid diagram is a **functional architecture flow** showing the sequence of operations a student performs (or a tool like `top` performs internally) to extract the six required pieces of information from the kernel via /proc.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["User runs shell command"] --> B{"Which info?"}
    B -->|CPU cores| C1["open /proc/cpuinfo"]
    C1 --> C2["count lines matching processor"]
    C2 --> C3["print N logical cores"]

    B -->|Memory| D1["open /proc/meminfo"]
    D1 --> D2["extract MemTotal and MemFree"]
    D2 --> D3["compute fraction MemFree/MemTotal"]
    D3 --> D4["print total and fraction"]

    B -->|Running and blocked procs| E1["open /proc/stat"]
    E1 --> E2["read procs_running and procs_blocked"]
    E2 --> E3["print counts"]

    B -->|Total forks| F1["open /proc/stat"]
    F1 --> F2["read processes field"]
    F2 --> F3["print fork count"]

    B -->|Context switches for PID p| G1["open /proc/p/status"]
    G1 --> G2["read voluntary and nonvoluntary"]
    G2 --> G3["sum to get total context switches"]
    G3 --> G4["print total for process p"]

    C3 --> H["Present results to user"]
    D4 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

## 4.3 Process State Machine (for Question (d))

A **process state diagram** clarifies the meaning of "running" and "blocked" as used in part (d) of the experiment.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : fork
    NEW --> READY : admitted
    READY --> RUNNING : scheduler dispatches
    RUNNING --> READY : preempted by scheduler
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED : waits on I/O or event
    BLOCKED --> READY : I/O or event completes
    RUNNING --> EXIT : process terminates
    EXIT --> [*] : cleanup complete
    note right of RUNNING
      R state
      count via procs_running
    end note
    note right of BLOCKED
      D and S states
      count via procs_blocked
    end note
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: From Kernel to User via /proc

This block diagram shows the **layered interaction** between the kernel data structures and a user-space shell command.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph KERNEL["Linux Kernel Ring 0"]
        K1["task_struct array"]
        K2["mm_struct per process"]
        K3["Global RAM counters"]
        K4["CPU runqueue"]
        K5["fork counter"]
    end
    subgraph VFS["Virtual File System Layer"]
        V1["procfs driver"]
        V2["inode operations table"]
    end
    subgraph USER["User Space Ring 3"]
        U1["cat /proc/meminfo"]
        U2["grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo"]
        U3["ps -e -o stat="]
        U4["awk over /proc/pid/status"]
    end
    K1 --> V1
    K2 --> V1
    K3 --> V1
    K4 --> V1
    K5 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> U1
    V2 --> U2
    V2 --> U3
    V2 --> U4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**What is the /proc file system? Why is it called a pseudo file system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The /proc file system is a virtual file system that provides an interface to the kernel data structures. **(1 mark)** It is called a pseudo file system because it does not exist on any physical storage device such as a hard disk; instead, its contents are generated dynamically in RAM by the kernel when they are read. **(1 mark)** Each file in /proc corresponds to a kernel variable, a hardware component, or a running process, and reading it returns live system information. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between `MemFree` and `MemAvailable` as reported by `/proc/meminfo`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`MemFree` represents the amount of physical RAM that is **completely unused** by the system at that instant — pages that are not allocated to any process or kernel buffer. **(1.5 marks)** `MemAvailable`, on the other hand, is an **estimate of memory available for starting new applications** without the system needing to swap; it includes a portion of `Cached` and `Buffers` that the kernel can reclaim quickly. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With suitable Linux commands and example outputs, explain how you would determine:
   (i) The number of CPU cores on your machine, and
   (ii) The total memory and fraction of free memory.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using `/proc/stat` and `/proc/loadavg`, explain how to obtain:
   (i) The number of processes currently in the running state, and
   (ii) The number of processes in the blocked state. Give sample outputs.

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Number of CPU cores:**

The `/proc/cpuinfo` file contains one block per logical processor. Each block begins with a line of the form `processor : N`. The count of such lines equals the number of logical CPU cores. **(1 mark)**

Command:
```bash
grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo
```

Sample output:
```
8
```

This indicates **8 logical CPUs**. **(1 mark)**

To get the physical core count, read `cpu cores` and `siblings` from the first block:

```bash
grep -E "^cpu cores|^siblings" /proc/cpuinfo | head -2
```

Sample output:
```
cpu cores       : 4
siblings        : 8
```

Physical cores = `siblings / (siblings / cpu cores)` = `8 / 2` = **4 physical cores**. **(2 marks)**

**(a)(ii) Total memory and fraction of free memory:**

The `/proc/meminfo` file exposes the relevant fields: **(1 mark)**

```bash
grep -E "MemTotal|MemFree|MemAvailable" /proc/meminfo
```

Sample output:
```
MemTotal:       16384000 kB
MemFree:         8234500 kB
MemAvailable:   11500000 kB
```

Computation:
$$
\text{Fraction Free} = \frac{8234500}{16384000} \approx 0.5027 = 50.27\%
$$

Command for fraction:
```bash
awk '/MemTotal/{t=$2}/MemFree/{f=$2}END{printf "Fraction Free = %.4f\n",f/t}' /proc/meminfo
```

Sample output:
```
Fraction Free = 0.5027
```

**(2 marks)** for the formula, the substitution, and the percentage.

**(b)(i) and (ii) Running and blocked processes:**

The `/proc/stat` file contains aggregate process counts in the `procs_running` and `procs_blocked` fields. **(1 mark)**

```bash
grep -E "procs_running|procs_blocked" /proc/stat
```

Sample output:
```
procs_running 2
procs_blocked 0
```

This means **2 processes are in the runnable (R) state** waiting for CPU, and **0 processes are in uninterruptible sleep (D state)** at that instant. **(1 mark)**

Cross-verification using `ps`:
```bash
ps -e -o stat= | sort | uniq -c
```

Sample output:
```
   1 I
 187 S
   3 R
   2 D
```

This shows **3 R-state processes** (one of which is the running one being measured) and **2 D-state processes** (waiting on I/O). **(2 marks)** The slight discrepancy in counts is because `grep` is non-atomic — the kernel updates `/proc/stat` between reads, while `ps` traverses all `/proc/<pid>/stat` files.

Alternative reading from `/proc/loadavg`: the fourth field `running/total` gives the running count directly:
```bash
cat /proc/loadavg
# Output: 0.45 0.60 0.55 1/234 5678
```
Here, **1 process is currently running, 234 total tasks** exist. **(1 mark)** for the alternative method and interpretation.

The remaining **2 marks** are for the explanation of R, S, D states and why blocked = D (uninterruptible sleep waiting on I/O).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the meaning of the `processes` field in `/proc/stat`. How is it incremented? What is its use in performance monitoring?

**(b) [7 marks]** For a given process with PID 4567, describe the procedure to find:
   (i) Its current state (R, S, D, etc.),
   (ii) The total number of context switches it has undergone since it started, and
   (iii) The breakdown between voluntary and non-voluntary context switches.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Meaning of `processes` field:**

The `processes` field in `/proc/stat` is a **monotonically increasing 64-bit counter** maintained by the Linux kernel. **(1 mark)** It records the **total number of task creations** that have occurred since the system was booted. A "task creation" occurs whenever the kernel's `do_fork()` function executes — that is, whenever a user program calls the `fork()`, `vfork()`, or `clone()` system calls (or when the kernel spawns a new kernel thread). **(2 marks)**

Command to read:
```bash
grep ^processes /proc/stat
```

Sample output:
```
processes 145678
```

This means **145,678 tasks have been created** since the last boot. **(1 mark)**

**How it is incremented:** Inside the kernel source `kernel/fork.c`, the function `copy_process()` is called by `do_fork()`. After successful task creation, the kernel executes `get_uid_ns(...)` and then increments a per-namespace counter `total_forks` via the `atomic_inc()` operation. This counter is exposed to user space as the `processes` field. **(1 mark)** Note: the value never decreases; even after processes exit, the count is not decremented.

**Use in performance monitoring:**
- Detecting **fork bombs** — sampling the field at small intervals and watching it skyrocket. **(1 mark)**
- Computing the **fork rate** $R_{\text{fork}} = \Delta N / \Delta t$, which gives the system load from process creation. **(1 mark)**
- Detecting misbehaving daemons (e.g., a web server spawning too many short-lived child processes).

**(b)(i) Current state of PID 4567:**

The current state character is the **3rd field** in `/proc/4567/stat`. **(1 mark)**

Command:
```bash
cat /proc/4567/stat | awk '{print $3}'
```

Sample output:
```
S
```

The `S` means the process is in **interruptible sleep**, waiting for an event (e.g., user input on its terminal, or data on a pipe). Other state characters and their meanings must be listed. **(1 mark)**

**(b)(ii) Total context switches for PID 4567:**

The `/proc/4567/status` file exposes two related fields. **(1 mark)**

Command:
```bash
grep -E "voluntary_ctxt_switches|nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches" /proc/4567/status
```

Sample output:
```
voluntary_ctxt_switches:        142
nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches:     89
```

The **total** is:
$$
C_{\text{total}}(4567) = V + N = 142 + 89 = 231 \text{ context switches}
$$

**(1 mark)** for the formula and **1 mark** for the arithmetic.

**(b)(iii) Breakdown:**

- **Voluntary (V = 142):** the process yielded the CPU itself by calling a blocking system call such as `read()` on an empty pipe, `wait()` on a child, `sleep()`, or `nanosleep()`. **(1 mark)**
- **Non-voluntary (N = 89):** the kernel preempted the process because its **time slice (sched_quantum)** expired, or because a higher-priority process became runnable and displaced it. **(1 mark)**

A high $N$ relative to $V$ indicates the process is **CPU-bound**; the kernel may need to lower its `nice` priority (i.e., give it a smaller share) to be fair to other processes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion (–1 mark):** Writing `MemTotal` in MB without noting the file reports **kB**, and 1 kB = 1024 B. Always write the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing `procs_running` with `procs_blocked` (–1 mark):** `procs_running` is the count of processes in the R state, not all active processes. A process doing disk I/O is *not* running — it is blocked (D or S).
> 3. **Treating `processes` as a current count (–1 to –2 marks):** It is a **cumulative** fork counter. A student writing "there are 145,678 processes" will lose marks; the correct phrasing is "145,678 tasks have been created since boot".
> 4. **Forgetting to add voluntary + non-voluntary (–1 mark):** When asked for "total context switches for a process", students often report only one of the two values.
> 5. **Wrong path to /proc entries (–1 mark):** Writing `/proc/cpu` or `/proc/ram` instead of the correct `/proc/cpuinfo` and `/proc/meminfo` will result in full credit loss for that sub-question.
> 6. **Ignoring the formula (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU examiners expect the fraction-of-free-memory formula written out symbolically, not just a number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **/proc is a virtual, RAM-resident file system** — never stored on disk, regenerated on every read.
- [ ] The **mount point** is always `/proc`; it is created by the kernel at boot.
- [ ] **Number of CPU cores** = count of `processor` lines in `/proc/cpuinfo` (logical cores).
- [ ] **Total memory** is in `/proc/meminfo` field `MemTotal` (in **kB**).
- [ ] **Fraction free** = `MemFree / MemTotal` (unitless, between 0 and 1).
- [ ] **MemAvailable** is a kernel estimate of memory usable for new applications (includes reclaimable cache).
- [ ] **`procs_running`** in `/proc/stat` = R-state processes (ready or currently executing).
- [ ] **`procs_blocked`** in `/proc/stat` = D-state processes (uninterruptible sleep, usually I/O wait).
- [ ] **`/proc/loadavg`** 4th field gives `running/total_tasks` as a quick read.
- [ ] **`processes` field in /proc/stat** = total forks + clones since boot (monotonically increasing).
- [ ] **Context switches for a specific process** are in `/proc/<PID>/status` as `voluntary_ctxt_switches` and `nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches`.
- [ ] **Voluntary** = process yielded CPU; **Non-voluntary** = kernel preempted.
- [ ] **Total context switches for a process** = voluntary + non-voluntary.
- [ ] Standard metrics: **Page size 4096 bytes**, **clock tick 100 Hz**, **PID range up to 4194304 on 64-bit Linux**.
- [ ] Tools like `top`, `ps`, `free`, `vmstat` are all frontends to /proc.
- [ ] Do not confuse **/proc** (process info) with **/sys** (device/driver info) — both are virtual but serve different purposes.
- [ ] For examination: always state the **path** you are reading, the **command** used, and the **unit** of the result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a simple program to print the system time and execute it. Then use the /proc file system to determine how long this program (in the strict sense, the corresponding process) ran in user and kernel modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL407 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a simple program to print the system time and execute it. Then use the /proc file system to determine how long this program (in the strict sense, the corresponding process) ran in user and kernel modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **POSIX (Portable Operating System Interface for Unix)** standard and the **Linux kernel architecture**, every running program is represented as a **process**, which is the fundamental unit of resource allocation and execution scheduling. The Linux kernel maintains detailed accounting information for every process, including the amount of CPU time consumed by the process in two distinct execution contexts:

- **User Mode Time (`utime`)**: The amount of time the CPU spent executing the process's instructions in **user space** (non-privileged mode). This includes arithmetic, library calls (e.g., `printf`), and application logic.
- **Kernel Mode Time (`stime`)**: The amount of time the CPU spent executing the process on behalf of **system calls**, interrupts, or kernel services (privileged mode). This includes I/O operations, memory allocation, and process control.

These statistics are exposed to userspace through the **/proc pseudo-filesystem** — a virtual, memory-mapped interface that provides a window into kernel data structures. The specific file `/proc/[pid]/stat` contains 52 space-delimited fields, where:
- **Field 14** = `utime` (user mode jiffies)
- **Field 15** = `stime` (kernel mode jiffies)

A **jiffy** is the internal time unit of the Linux kernel, equivalent to one **clock tick**, governed by the constant `CLK_TCK` (defined in `<unistd.h>` and retrieved via `sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK)`). On virtually all modern Linux distributions, **CLK_TCK = 100**, meaning **1 second = 100 jiffies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The /proc filesystem is a "virtual" filesystem (procfs) that is generated dynamically by the kernel at runtime. It does **not** occupy any actual disk storage — it resides entirely in RAM and is created/destroyed as the system boots and shuts down.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are a **chef working in a kitchen (the CPU)**. You have two types of tasks:

1. **Cooking your recipe (User Mode)**: Mixing ingredients, chopping vegetables, following your own cookbook — this is *your* work, not requiring any special permission.
2. **Asking the kitchen manager for help (Kernel Mode)**: Every time you need the oven preheated, a special pan, or to call the supplier, the kitchen manager (kernel) must step in and handle the request.

Now, the **restaurant owner** wants to know how much of the chef's day was spent cooking versus asking the manager for help. To track this, the owner installs a **stopwatch** on two clocks:
- A **green clock** that runs only when the chef is actively cooking (utime).
- A **red clock** that runs only when the manager is helping (stime).

In Linux, this "stopwatch" is the kernel's process scheduler, and the two clocks' readings are stored in `/proc/[pid]/stat`. The restaurant owner (you, the student) can read these clock values at any time and convert them to seconds by dividing by `CLK_TCK` (100).

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters

| Concept | Real-World Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **System Time** | Timestamping logs, scheduling cron jobs, network protocol synchronization (NTP) |
| **Process Time Accounting** | Performance profiling, identifying CPU-bound vs I/O-bound programs |
| **/proc Filesystem** | Used by tools like `top`, `htop`, `ps`, `pgrep`, system monitors, and container runtimes (Docker, Kubernetes cgroups) |

> [!NOTE]
> The `/proc` filesystem is the **backbone of Linux observability**. Every system monitoring tool you have ever used (`top`, `htop`, `ps`, `gnome-system-monitor`) ultimately reads from `/proc` files. Mastering this experiment gives you direct access to the same data these tools use.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The /proc Filesystem — Internal Architecture

The Linux kernel implements `/proc` as a **virtual filesystem** registered via the VFS (Virtual File System Switch) layer. Unlike `ext4` or `xfs` (which read/write from disk), procfs generates file contents on-the-fly by invoking kernel callbacks when a user process calls `read()`.

### 2.1.1 Directory Structure Relevant to This Experiment

```
/proc/
├── [pid]/              ← Numeric directories, one per active process
│   ├── stat            ← Process status (52 fields, space-delimited)
│   ├── status          ← Human-readable equivalent of stat
│   ├── cmdline         ← Command-line arguments
│   ├── cwd -> /home/...← Symbolic link to current working directory
│   ├── exe -> /usr/bin/... ← Symbolic link to executable file
│   └── statm           ← Memory usage information
├── self -> /proc/[current_pid]  ← Self-referencing symbolic link
├── uptime              ← System uptime and idle time
├── loadavg             ← Load average over 1, 5, 15 minutes
└── meminfo             ← Memory usage statistics
```

### 2.1.2 Decoding /proc/[pid]/stat

The `/proc/[pid]/stat` file contains a single line with 52 whitespace-separated fields. The most important fields for this experiment are:

| Field # | Name | Symbol | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PID | `pid` | Process ID | Integer |
| 2 | Comm | `comm` | Executable filename in parentheses | String |
| 3 | State | `state` | Process state (R/S/D/Z/T) | Char |
| 4 | PPID | `ppid` | Parent process ID | Integer |
| 14 | **utime** | `utime` | **Time scheduled in user mode** | **Clock ticks (jiffies)** |
| 15 | **stime** | `stime` | **Time scheduled in kernel mode** | **Clock ticks (jiffies)** |
| 22 | starttime | `starttime` | Time process started after boot | Clock ticks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Detail:** Field 2 (`comm`) is enclosed in parentheses, and the executable name itself may contain spaces or parentheses. This means you **cannot** simply split the line by spaces and take index `[1]`. You must find the **last** closing parenthesis `)` to correctly separate the comm field from the rest.

## 2.2 Clock Ticks and Time Conversion

The Linux kernel's scheduler fires the **timer interrupt** at a fixed frequency configured at kernel compile time (`CONFIG_HZ`). On most modern systems, this is **1000 Hz** for hardware timer interrupts, but the **user-visible jiffy rate** (for accounting) is standardized at `CLK_TCK = 100`.

$$ \text{Time in seconds} = \frac{\text{Time in jiffies}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} $$

In C code:
```c
#include <unistd.h>
long ticks_per_sec = sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK);  // Returns 100 on Linux
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total CPU Time | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{kernel}}$ | Sum of user and kernel mode time |
| 2 | User Time (seconds) | $T_{\text{user}} = \dfrac{\text{utime}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}}$ | utime is in jiffies from `/proc/[pid]/stat` field 14 |
| 3 | Kernel Time (seconds) | $T_{\text{kernel}} = \dfrac{\text{stime}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}}$ | stime is in jiffies from `/proc/[pid]/stat` field 15 |
| 4 | Total Time (seconds) | $T_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\text{utime} + \text{stime}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}}$ | Combined time spent on CPU |
| 5 | CLK_TCK Constant | $\text{CLK\_TCK} = \text{sysconf}(\_\text{SC\_CLK\_TCK}) = 100$ | Standard value on x86_64 Linux |
| 6 | Real (Wall-Clock) Time | $T_{\text{real}} = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | Elapsed time as seen by a stopwatch |
| 7 | CPU Utilization Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{kernel}}}{T_{\text{real}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of wall time spent on CPU |
| 8 | Jiffy Definition | $1 \text{ jiffy} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} \text{ seconds} = 0.01 \text{ s}$ (for CLK_TCK=100) | Basic unit of kernel time accounting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Time vs CPU Time:** A process reading from a slow disk may take 5 seconds in wall-clock time but only use 0.01 seconds of CPU time (because it spends most of its time blocked in `I/O wait`). The `/proc` filesystem tells you **CPU time**, not wall time. To measure wall time, use `time ./program` from the shell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Performance Engineering** | Profilers (gprof, perf, Valgrind) sample `/proc/[pid]/stat` to attribute CPU usage to specific functions |
| **Container Orchestration** | Docker and Kubernetes use `/proc` to enforce CPU quotas via cgroups v2 (`cpu.max`) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion detection systems monitor `/proc` for anomalous processes (e.g., hidden rootkits with modified utime) |
| **Embedded Systems** | RTOS engineers track kernel vs user time to ensure hard real-time deadlines are met |
| **Cloud Cost Optimization** | AWS/GCP bill based on CPU-seconds consumed; accurate measurement via `/proc/stat` enables cost reduction |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Code Walkthrough & Derivations

## 3.1 Algorithm / Experimental Procedure

The experiment follows a precise sequence of operations. Each step must be executed in the order specified.

### **Step 1: Write the C Program**

Create a file named `sys_time.c` with the following content. This program prints the system time, performs a CPU-bound computation, and remains measurable during its lifetime.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>

int main(void) {
    // ---- (a) Print current system time ----
    time_t raw_time;
    struct tm *time_info;

    time(&raw_time);
    time_info = localtime(&raw_time);

    printf("=== SYSTEM TIME EXPERIMENT ===\n");
    printf("Current system time : %s", asctime(time_info));
    printf("Process PID         : %d\n", getpid());
    printf("Parent PID          : %d\n", getppid());
    fflush(stdout);   // Ensure output is flushed before heavy work

    // ---- (b) CPU-bound work to generate measurable utime ----
    volatile long long sum = 0;
    long long iterations = 200000000LL;   // 2 x 10^8 loop iterations

    printf("Starting CPU-bound loop (2x10^8 iterations)...\n");
    fflush(stdout);

    for (long long i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
        sum += i;                          // Pure arithmetic in user space
    }

    printf("Loop complete. Sum = %lld\n", sum);

    // ---- (c) Kernel-mode work via write() system call ----
    // This generates measurable stime (kernel mode time)
    FILE *fp = fopen("/tmp/result.txt", "w");
    if (fp != NULL) {
        fprintf(fp, "Sum = %lld\n", sum);
        fclose(fp);
    }

    printf("=== PROGRAM EXITING ===\n");
    return 0;
}
```

### **Step 2: Compile the Program**

Open a terminal and execute the following command:

```bash
gcc -O0 -o sys_time sys_time.c
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `-O0` flag disables compiler optimization. This is **deliberate** — with `-O2` or `-O3`, the compiler may eliminate the entire loop (since `sum` is `volatile`, it cannot be removed, but other optimizations could affect timing).

### **Step 3: Execute and Capture PID**

There are two valid approaches. **Approach A** (Background execution) is preferred for clean measurements.

**Approach A — Background Execution:**
```bash
./sys_time &
[1] 12345    # The terminal prints the PID; record this number
```

**Approach B — Simultaneous Monitoring with watch:**
```bash
./sys_time &   # Start in background
PID=$!         # Capture PID via shell variable
echo "PID is: $PID"
```

### **Step 4: Read /proc/[pid]/stat**

In the same or a second terminal, while the program is still running, execute:

```bash
cat /proc/12345/stat
```

The output will be a single line similar to:

```
12345 (sys_time) R 6789 12345 6789 34816 12345 ...
   |     |       |   |
   |     |       |   +-- Field 4: PPID
   |     |       +------ Field 3: State (R = Running, S = Sleeping)
   |     +-------------- Field 2: comm (in parentheses)
   +-------------------- Field 1: PID
```

To safely extract the utime and stime values regardless of the executable name containing special characters, use the **`awk` or `cut` approach with the closing parenthesis as a delimiter:**

```bash
cat /proc/12345/stat | awk '{print $14, $15}'
```

**Output:**
```
125 8
```

> [!WARNING]
> Using simple `cut -d' '` will fail if the `comm` field contains spaces! Always use `awk` or parse with a robust script. The `comm` field in field position `[1]` (zero-indexed) of the split result is unreliable.

### **Step 5: Convert Jiffies to Seconds**

Apply the conversion formula:

$$ T_{\text{user}} = \frac{\text{utime}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} = \frac{125}{100} = 1.25 \text{ seconds} $$

$$ T_{\text{kernel}} = \frac{\text{stime}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} = \frac{8}{100} = 0.08 \text{ seconds} $$

$$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{kernel}} = 1.25 + 0.08 = 1.33 \text{ seconds} $$

### **Step 6: Verify with `time` Command**

As a cross-check, run the program with the shell's built-in `time`:

```bash
time ./sys_time
```

**Expected output:**
```
=== SYSTEM TIME EXPERIMENT ===
Current system time : Mon Jan 15 14:23:45 2024
Process PID         : 12350
...
real    0m1.520s    ← Wall clock time
user    0m1.250s    ← Matches our utime/CLK_TCK = 1.25s ✓
sys     0m0.080s    ← Matches our stime/CLK_TCK = 0.08s ✓
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shell `time` command and our `/proc` calculation should agree within a small tolerance (typically ±0.05s) due to sampling delay between `cat /proc/.../stat` and the program's actual completion.

## 3.2 Mathematical Verification — Worked Example

### Given Data
From a sample run of `sys_time`, the `/proc/[pid]/stat` file returned:

```
13001 (sys_time) R 12000 13001 12000 34816 ...
```

Parsing the 14th and 15th whitespace-separated fields:
- `utime` = 247 jiffies
- `stime` = 13 jiffies
- System `CLK_TCK` = 100

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the raw jiffy values from the procfs output.

$$\text{utime}_{\text{raw}} = 247 \text{ jiffies}, \quad \text{stime}_{\text{raw}} = 13 \text{ jiffies}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the conversion constant `CLK_TCK = 100`.

$$T_{\text{user}} = \frac{\text{utime}_{\text{raw}}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} = \frac{247}{100} = 2.47 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate kernel time.

$$T_{\text{kernel}} = \frac{\text{stime}_{\text{raw}}}{\text{CLK\_TCK}} = \frac{13}{100} = 0.13 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute total CPU time.

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{kernel}} = 2.47 + 0.13 = 2.60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5:** If the wall-clock real time was 2.75s, compute CPU utilization.

$$\eta = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{real}}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.60}{2.75} \times 100\% \approx 94.55\%$$

The remaining **5.45%** represents time spent in scheduler overhead, I/O wait, or context switching.

## 3.3 Edge Cases and Robust Parsing

### 3.3.1 Handling Spaces in Command Name

If a malicious or poorly-named process is named `my (weird) program`, naive space-splitting will fail. **Robust awk solution:**

```bash
cat /proc/$PID/stat | awk -F') ' '{print $2}' | awk '{print $13, $14}'
```

The `-F') '` splits on the closing parenthesis (which is guaranteed to appear only once), and then the second `awk` extracts fields 14 and 15 from the remaining portion.

### 3.3.2 Self-Referencing with `/proc/self`

A process can inspect its own statistics without knowing its PID:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>

int main(void) {
    // /proc/self/stat automatically resolves to /proc/[current_pid]/stat
    FILE *fp = fopen("/proc/self/stat", "r");
    if (fp == NULL) { perror("fopen"); return 1; }

    unsigned long utime, stime;
    // Read 14 fields, then utime (14th), then stime (15th)
    fscanf(fp, "%*d %*s %*c %*d %*d %*d %*d %*d %*u %*u %*u %*u %*u %lu %lu",
           &utime, &stime);
    fclose(fp);

    printf("Self-reported utime: %lu jiffies (%.2f s)\n",
           utime, utime / (double)sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK));
    printf("Self-reported stime: %lu jiffies (%.2f s)\n",
           stime, stime / (double)sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK));
    return 0;
}
```

This is a **powerful technique** used in performance profilers and self-monitoring daemons.

## 3.4 Summary of Output Values to Record in Lab Record

| S.No. | Metric | Value (from /proc) | Value (in seconds) | Cross-check (via `time` cmd) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `utime` (jiffies) | 247 | 2.47 s | `user 0m2.47s` |
| 2 | `stime` (jiffies) | 13 | 0.13 s | `sys 0m0.13s` |
| 3 | Total CPU time | 260 jiffies | 2.60 s | `user + sys` |
| 4 | Wall time | — | 2.75 s | `real 0m2.75s` |
| 5 | CLK_TCK | 100 | 100 ticks/s | `sysconf` result |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the /proc filesystem in Linux? Why is it called a "virtual" filesystem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The /proc filesystem is a **virtual (pseudo) filesystem** in Linux that provides an interface to kernel data structures. It is called "virtual" because:
1. **(1 mark)** It does not occupy any real disk space — its contents are generated dynamically in RAM by kernel functions.
2. **(1 mark)** It is created at system boot and destroyed at shutdown, existing only while the system is running.
3. **(1 mark)** It exposes internal kernel information (process details, memory, hardware config) as files that can be read using standard file operations like `open()`, `read()`, etc.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**Explain the difference between `utime` and `stime` in the `/proc/[pid]/stat` file. How are they measured?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **`utime` (User Mode Time) (1 mark)**: The amount of CPU time the process has spent executing in **user space** (non-privileged mode). This includes the application's own code and library calls.
- **`stime` (System/Kernel Mode Time) (1 mark)**: The amount of CPU time spent executing **system calls** on behalf of the process in kernel space.
- **Measurement (1 mark)**: Both are measured in **clock ticks (jiffies)**. The number of jiffies per second is defined by `CLK_TCK`, which is typically **100** on Linux. To convert to seconds: `seconds = jiffies / CLK_TCK`.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Write a C program that prints the current system time using the `time()` and `localtime()` library functions. Explain each function call. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how you would use the `/proc` filesystem to determine how long the program in part (a) ran in user and kernel mode. Include the relevant formulas and the value of `CLK_TCK`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**C Program (5 marks for code + 2 marks for explanation):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <time.h>

int main(void) {
    time_t current_time;          // (1) Variable to hold raw time
    struct tm *time_info;         // (2) Pointer to broken-down time structure

    time(&current_time);          // (3) Get current calendar time (seconds since epoch)
    time_info = localtime(&current_time);  // (4) Convert to local time structure

    printf("Current System Time: %s", asctime(time_info));  // (5) Print formatted time

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Key Functions (2 marks):**

1. **`time_t time(time_t *tloc)` (1 mark)**: This standard C library function returns the current calendar time as the number of seconds elapsed since the **Unix Epoch** (00:00:00 UTC, January 1, 1970). The value is returned both via the return value and stored in `*tloc` if `tloc` is not NULL. The header `<time.h>` must be included, and `time_t` is typically a typedef for `long` or `long long`.

2. **`struct tm *localtime(const time_t *timep)` (1 mark)**: This function converts the raw `time_t` value into a `struct tm` representing **local time** (adjusted for the system's timezone). The returned structure contains fields like `tm_year`, `tm_mon`, `tm_mday`, `tm_hour`, `tm_min`, `tm_sec`. The `asctime()` function then converts this structure back into a human-readable string.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Execution and PID Capture (2 marks)**
Compile the program and execute it in the background:
```bash
gcc -o systime systime.c
./systime &
[1] 4251   # PID is 4251
```

**Step 2: Reading /proc/PID/stat (2 marks)**
While the program is running, read its process statistics:
```bash
cat /proc/4251/stat
```
Output: `4251 (systime) R 4000 4251 4000 34816 4251 ... 250 12 ...`

**Step 3: Extracting utime and stime (2 marks)**
The 14th field is `utime` and the 15th field is `stime` (in jiffies):
- `utime = 250` jiffies
- `stime = 12` jiffies

**Step 4: Conversion using Formulas (1 mark)**
Using `CLK_TCK = 100` (the standard value on Linux, obtainable via `sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK)`):

$$T_{\text{user}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{\text{kernel}} = \frac{12}{100} = 0.12 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 2.5 + 0.12 = 2.62 \text{ seconds}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is the `/proc` filesystem? List at least five important files/directories inside `/proc` and explain their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program that reads its own `/proc/self/stat` file, extracts `utime` and `stime`, and prints the user and kernel mode time in seconds. Explain the parsing logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The `/proc` filesystem (procfs) is a virtual filesystem in Linux that exposes kernel and process information as a hierarchy of files. It is mounted at boot time at the `/proc` directory and is implemented in-memory by the kernel. It serves as the primary interface for tools like `ps`, `top`, and `htop` to retrieve system information.

**Five Important Files/Directories (1 mark each):**

1. **`/proc/[pid]/stat`**: Contains detailed process status (52 fields) including state, parent PID, priority, and crucially `utime` (field 14) and `stime` (field 15).

2. **`/proc/[pid]/status`**: A human-readable version of `stat`, with key-value pairs like `Name:`, `State:`, `Uid:`, `Threads:`, `VmRSS:`.

3. **`/proc/[pid]/cmdline`**: Contains the complete command line that started the process, with arguments separated by null bytes.

4. **`/proc/meminfo`**: Global memory statistics including `MemTotal`, `MemFree`, `MemAvailable`, `Buffers`, `Cached`, `SwapTotal`.

5. **`/proc/cpuinfo`**: Information about the CPU(s) including model name, clock speed, cache size, and flags (features supported).

6. **`/proc/uptime`**: Two values — system uptime in seconds and amount of time spent in idle.

7. **`/proc/loadavg`**: Load average over the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes, plus running/total processes.

8. **`/proc/self`**: A symbolic link to the `/proc` directory of the process currently accessing it.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**Complete C Program (5 marks for code, 2 marks for explanation):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>

int main(void) {
    FILE *fp;
    unsigned long utime_jiffies = 0, stime_jiffies = 0;
    long clk_tck;
    int field;
    char comm[256];
    char state;

    /* Open the /proc/self/stat file */
    fp = fopen("/proc/self/stat", "r");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening /proc/self/stat");
        return EXIT_FAILURE;
    }

    /*
     * Parse the file. Format: PID (comm) state ppid pgrp ...
     * Fields 1-13 must be read and discarded.
     * Field 14 is utime, Field 15 is stime.
     */
    fscanf(fp, "%*d %s %c %*d %*d %*d %*d %*d %*u %*u %*u %*u %*u %lu %lu",
           comm, &state, &utime_jiffies, &stime_jiffies);

    fclose(fp);

    /* Get the system clock tick rate */
    clk_tck = sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK);
    if (clk_tck == -1) clk_tck = 100;  /* Safe default for Linux */

    /* Convert jiffies to seconds */
    double user_sec   = (double)utime_jiffies / (double)clk_tck;
    double kernel_sec = (double)stime_jiffies / (double)clk_tck;

    /* Display the results */
    printf("Process Name       : %s\n", comm);
    printf("Process State      : %c\n", state);
    printf("CLK_TCK            : %ld ticks/sec\n", clk_tck);
    printf("utime (raw)        : %lu jiffies\n", utime_jiffies);
    printf("stime (raw)        : %lu jiffies\n", stime_jiffies);
    printf("User Mode Time     : %.4f seconds\n", user_sec);
    printf("Kernel Mode Time   : %.4f seconds\n", kernel_sec);
    printf("Total CPU Time     : %.4f seconds\n", user_sec + kernel_sec);

    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}
```

**Explanation of Parsing Logic (2 marks):**

1. **`/proc/self` is a symbolic link** (1 mark) that always points to the current process's PID directory. This eliminates the need to call `getpid()` and construct the path manually. It is particularly useful in multi-threaded applications and libraries.

2. **The `%*` conversion specifier in `fscanf` (1 mark)** tells the C library to read a value according to the format but **discard it** (assign to no variable). For example, `%*d` reads an integer and throws it away. This allows us to skip the first 13 fields efficiently and capture only the two we need (`utime` and `stime`).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by CLK_TCK (1-2 marks lost):** Students often report `utime = 247 jiffies` and `stime = 13 jiffies` as the final answer. The examiner expects the **converted seconds** value. Always apply the formula.
>
> 2. **Incorrect field indexing (2-3 marks lost):** `/proc/[pid]/stat` has fields numbered starting from 1, not 0. The 14th field is `utime`. If you use `awk`, remember that `awk` indexes from 1 as well. Off-by-one errors are extremely common.
>
> 3. **Not handling spaces in comm field (2 marks lost):** The `comm` field in field 2 is enclosed in parentheses, and the executable name can contain spaces. Naive space-splitting breaks. Use `awk` with `)` as the delimiter, or use `cut` carefully.
>
> 4. **Confusing `time` command output (1 mark lost):** The shell `time` command prints `user` and `sys` times, which correspond to `utime` and `stime` of the **entire process tree**, not just the immediate program. For a single C program, they should match closely, but be aware of the difference.
>
> 5. **Not recording the PID properly (1 mark lost):** You must **explicitly write down the PID** of the running process in your lab record. Examiners expect to see: "PID = 4251, /proc/4251/stat was read."
>
> 6. **Using `printf` without `fflush` (subtle issue):** When running in the background with `&`, stdout is buffered. If your program crashes or finishes before the buffer is flushed, output is lost. Always call `fflush(stdout)` after `printf` in background-executed programs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📋 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **`/proc` is a virtual filesystem** — generated in RAM, no disk storage, exists only at runtime.
- [x] **`/proc/[pid]/stat` contains 52 fields** in a single space-separated line.
- [x] **Field 14 = `utime`** (user mode jiffies); **Field 15 = `stime`** (kernel mode jiffies).
- [x] **`CLK_TCK = 100`** on standard Linux x86_64 systems (verify with `sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK)`).
- [x] **Conversion formula**: $T_{\text{seconds}} = \text{jiffies} \div \text{CLK\_TCK}$
- [x] **Total CPU time** = $T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{kernel}}$
- [x] **Wall-clock time** (from `time` command) ≥ **CPU time** (from `/proc`) — never the reverse.
- [x] **Parsing tip**: Use `awk '{print $14, $15}'` to extract utime/stime robustly.
- [x] **`/proc/self` is a self-referencing link** to the current process's `/proc/[pid]` directory.
- [x] **`time()` returns seconds since Unix Epoch** (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC).
- [x] **`localtime()` converts `time_t` to a `struct tm` in local timezone.**
- [x] **`asctime()` converts `struct tm` to a human-readable string** like `"Mon Jan 15 14:23:45 2024"`.
- [x] **Required headers for time functions**: `<time.h>` (for `time`, `localtime`, `asctime`).
- [x] **Required headers for process IDs**: `<unistd.h>` (for `getpid`, `getppid`) and `<sys/types.h>`.
- [x] **Required header for `sysconf`**: `<unistd.h>`.

### 🎯 Key Takeaways for KTU Practical Exam

1. **The experiment is reproducible**: Compile → Run in background → Read `/proc/[pid]/stat` → Convert to seconds.
2. **Three values to record**: PID, raw jiffies, time in seconds (with units).
3. **One cross-verification**: Always run `time ./program` to confirm your `/proc` calculation.
4. **One key awareness**: The numbers may not match *exactly* due to measurement timing — note the tolerance in your observation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If asked "How is CPU time different from wall-clock time?" in viva, answer: *"CPU time is the time the CPU actually spent executing instructions of the process, while wall-clock time is the total elapsed time as measured by a stopwatch, which includes time the process spent sleeping, waiting for I/O, or not scheduled."*

---

topics.title: Create a new process using a fork system call. Print the parent and child process IDs. Use the pstree command to find the process tree for the child process starting from the init process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL407 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create a new process using a fork system call. Print the parent and child process IDs. Use the pstree command to find the process tree for the child process starting from the init process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Process

A **process** is the basic unit of execution within an operating system. It is an instance of a program in execution, comprising the program code, its current activity (represented by the program counter and processor registers), a process stack, a data section, a heap, and a unique identifier known as the **Process Identifier (PID)**. In the Linux/Unix operating system context relevant to the KTU 2024 syllabus, processes are managed by the kernel through a control block called the **task_struct**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of the `fork()` System Call

The `fork()` system call is the fundamental POSIX-defined mechanism for creating a new process in Unix-like operating systems (including Linux). It duplicates the calling process, producing a brand-new child process that is an almost exact replica of the parent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** `fork()` is declared in `<unistd.h>`. It takes no arguments and returns a value of type `pid_t`. After a successful `fork()` call, two processes continue execution from the instruction immediately following the `fork()` call. The new process is called the **child**, and the original process is called the **parent**.

The signature of the function is:

$$fork() : \text{pid\_t} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}$$

Where the return value distinguishes the parent from the child:

$$\text{Return Value} = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if fork fails (error occurred)} \\ 0 & \text{in the child process (newly created)} \\ > 0 & \text{in the parent process, value is the PID of the newly created child} \end{cases}$$

## 1.3 The `pstree` Command

The `pstree` command is a Unix utility that displays the running processes in the form of a tree diagram. The tree's root is either `init` (PID 1) or the process specified by the user. Each process is shown as a node connected to its parent by a line, providing a clear visual representation of the parent-child relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **`init` Process (PID 1):** This is the very first process started by the kernel during system boot. All user-space processes are direct or indirect descendants of `init` (or `systemd` in modern systems). It serves as the ancestor of every process in the system.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photocopy Machine"

Imagine you are writing an exam in a large hall. You are the **parent process**.

1. **The Decision to `fork()`**: You take a blank answer sheet and run it through a magical photocopy machine. This is the `fork()` system call.
2. **The Result**: Suddenly, there are two identical answer sheets — one with your name (the **parent**), and one that is a fresh copy (the **child**). Both are now in the hall, both look identical, but they are two different people writing the exam from this point forward.
3. **The Return Value**: The photocopy machine beeps and shows a number. For you (the original), it shows the **roll number of the new copy** (child's PID). For the new copy, it shows **0** (telling the child, "you are the new one").
4. **Independent Lives**: From this moment, the two can write different answers, finish at different times, and are tracked separately by the invigilator (the **Operating System kernel**).
5. **The `pstree` View**: The invigilator's register book has a family tree. If you ask, "Show me the tree of processes starting from `init`," the invigilator can draw a genealogy chart showing how your process and the child's process both branch out from the system's very first invigilator (`init`).

## 1.5 Key Process Identifiers (PIDs)

| Identifier | Type | Definition | KTU Standard Range (Linux) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PID (Process ID)** | `pid_t` | Unique identifier of a process | **1 to 32768** (default) |
| **PPID (Parent PID)** | `pid_t` | PID of the process that created this one | Always less than child's PID in a single fork |
| **PGID (Process Group ID)** | `pid_t` | Identifier of the process group | Group leader's PID |
| **SID (Session ID)** | `pid_t` | Identifier of the session | Session leader's PID |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember the three fundamental calls used together with `fork()`:
> - `pid_t getpid(void)` → returns **current process's PID**
> - `pid_t getppid(void)` → returns **parent's PID**
> - `pid_t getpgid(pid_t pid)` → returns **process group ID**

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of `fork()` — Step by Step

The `fork()` system call follows a deterministic sequence of kernel operations:

1. **Kernel Invocation**: The user-space process invokes `fork()` via the system call interface. The CPU transitions from **User Mode** to **Kernel Mode** (a privilege escalation controlled by the **trap/syscall** instruction).

2. **Process Descriptor Allocation**: The kernel allocates a new `task_struct` (the kernel's process control block) and assigns a new unique **PID** to the child using the kernel's PID allocator (typically a bitmap-based or IDR-based mechanism in modern Linux).

3. **Resource Duplication**:
   - A copy of the parent's **address space** is created.
   - The parent's **file descriptor table** is duplicated (both processes now share open file offsets).
   - The parent's **signal handlers**, **environment variables**, and **current working directory** are copied.
   - The **stack, heap, and data segments** are duplicated.

4. **Return Value Assignment**:
   - The **child** receives a return value of `0`.
   - The **parent** receives the **PID of the child** (a positive integer).

5. **Scheduling**: Both processes are placed in the **run queue** (`TASK_RUNNING` state in Linux terminology). The scheduler decides which one runs first — this is non-deterministic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Copy-on-Write (COW) Optimization:** In modern Linux kernels, `fork()` does not physically duplicate the entire memory. Instead, the page tables of both processes point to the same physical pages, marked as **read-only**. Only when either process attempts to **write** to a page does a **page fault** occur, triggering the kernel to actually copy that page. This drastically reduces the cost of `fork()` and makes it efficient.

## 2.2 Process States After `fork()`

Immediately after `fork()` returns, the two processes move through these states:

$$\text{Created} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Waiting} \rightarrow \text{Terminated}$$

The exact ordering of execution between parent and child is determined by the **CPU scheduler** and is **non-deterministic** without explicit synchronization (e.g., `wait()`, `sleep()`).

## 2.3 The `pstree` Command — Deep Analysis

The `pstree` utility reads the process information from the `/proc` filesystem (specifically, `/proc/[pid]/status`) and constructs a hierarchical tree.

### 2.3.1 Common `pstree` Options

| Option | Effect | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `pstree` (no args) | Shows the entire process tree starting from `init` | Default system view |
| `pstree -p` | Shows **PIDs** in parentheses next to process names | Required for KTU lab records |
| `pstree <PID>` | Shows the process tree rooted at the given PID | Finding a specific sub-tree |
| `pstree -s <PID>` | Shows the **ancestors** of the given PID (path to init) | Tracing a process back to init |
| `pstree -a` | Shows command-line arguments | Detailed debugging |
| `pstree -h` | Highlights the current process | Visual aid |
| `pstree -p <PID>` | Shows the tree with PIDs starting from a specific PID | **Standard KTU observation** |

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `fork()` return in child | $0$ | The child always gets 0 |
| `fork()` return in parent | $\text{child\_pid} > 0$ | Parent gets the new child's PID |
| `fork()` failure | $-1$ | Returned to the calling process, no child created |
| Maximum PID | $\text{pid\_max} - 1$ | Default 32767 on 32-bit, 4194304 on 64-bit |
| Child's PPID | $\text{parent's PID}$ | Always equal to parent's PID |
| Generation depth | $d$ | Number of forks from `init` to process |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** The `pstree` command for the **child process** means you must first **execute the program**, note the **child's PID** from its output, and then run `pstree -p <child_PID>` in a separate terminal to see the child's process tree.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `fork()` mechanism is the foundation for:
- **Web servers** (e.g., Apache's `prefork` MPM) — each incoming connection is handled by a forked child process.
- **Shell command execution** — when you type `ls` in a terminal, the shell forks and the child `exec()`s the `ls` binary.
- **Parallel computing** — computationally intensive tasks can be parallelized by forking worker processes.
- **Process supervision systems** — `init`, `systemd`, and `supervisord` use fork to spawn and monitor services.

## 2.5 Algorithm Flow (Pseudocode)

The standard pattern taught in KTU labs:

```
pid_t pid;

pid = fork();

if (pid < 0) {
    // ERROR: fork failed
    fprintf(stderr, "Fork Failed");
    exit(1);
} else if (pid == 0) {
    // CHILD PROCESS BLOCK
    printf("Child: PID = %d, PPID = %d\n", getpid(), getppid());
} else {
    // PARENT PROCESS BLOCK
    printf("Parent: PID = %d, Child PID = %d\n", getpid(), pid);
    wait(NULL);  // Optional: wait for child to finish
}
```

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Code Analysis, Execution Flow & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Complete Lab Program (Standard KTU 2024 Version)

```c
/*
 * Program : Process creation using fork()
 * Course  : PCCSL407 - Operating Systems Lab
 * Scheme  : KTU 2024
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/types.h>

int main(void) {
    pid_t pid;

    printf("Before fork(): Process started.\n");
    printf("Current PID = %d, PPID = %d\n", getpid(), getppid());

    /* ---- THE FORK CALL ---- */
    pid = fork();

    /* ---- ERROR CHECK ---- */
    if (pid < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "fork() failed!\n");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    /* ---- CHILD PROCESS BLOCK ---- */
    else if (pid == 0) {
        printf("\n--- CHILD PROCESS ---\n");
        printf("Child  PID  = %d\n", getpid());
        printf("Parent PID  = %d (PPID)\n", getppid());
        printf("fork() return = %d\n", pid);

        /* Keep child alive for pstree observation */
        printf("Child sleeping for 30 seconds for pstree observation...\n");
        sleep(30);
        printf("Child exiting.\n");
        exit(0);
    }

    /* ---- PARENT PROCESS BLOCK ---- */
    else {
        printf("\n--- PARENT PROCESS ---\n");
        printf("Parent PID     = %d\n", getpid());
        printf("Child  PID     = %d (returned by fork)\n", pid);
        printf("fork() return  = %d\n", pid);

        /* Keep parent alive briefly */
        printf("Parent sleeping for 10 seconds...\n");
        sleep(10);
        printf("Parent exiting.\n");
    }

    return 0;
}
```

## 3.2 Compilation and Execution Steps (Exhaustive)

Step 1 — Open a terminal in the Linux lab environment (Ubuntu/CentOS/Fedora).

Step 2 — Save the file as `fork_demo.c` using any editor (`gedit`, `vim`, `nano`).

Step 3 — Compile the program using `gcc` with strict warnings enabled:

$$gcc\ -Wall\ -o\ fork\_demo\ fork\_demo.c$$

Step 4 — Execute the program in the **foreground**:

$$./fork\_demo$$

Step 5 — Note the **child's PID** printed by the program (e.g., `Child PID = 4512`).

Step 6 — Open **another terminal** while the child is still sleeping.

Step 7 — Run the `pstree` command with the child's PID:

$$pstree\ -p\ 4512$$

```
--- PARENT PROCESS ---
Parent PID     = 4511
Child  PID     = 4512 (returned by fork)
fork() return  = 4512

--- CHILD PROCESS ---
Child  PID  = 4512
Parent PID  = 4511 (PPID)
fork() return = 0

```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The order of "Parent Process" and "Child Process" output blocks is **non-deterministic**. It depends on the OS scheduler. Sometimes the child prints first, sometimes the parent.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Identification Table

For the sample run above, the analytical matrix is:

| Process | PID | PPID | fork() Return | State During sleep(30) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parent (Bash forked user) | 4511 | 2100 (shell) | 4512 (child's PID) | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` (sleeping) |
| Child (newly created) | 4512 | 4511 (parent) | 0 | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` (sleeping) |

## 3.5 Using `pstree` to Trace the Child Back to `init`

The KTU syllabus specifically requires showing the path from the child process up to `init` (PID 1).

**Command:**
$$pstree\ -s\ 4512$$

**Typical Output (truncated):**
```
systemd(1)───bash(2100)───fork_demo(4511)───fork_demo(4512)
```

This output reads as:
- The ancestor of all processes is `systemd` (PID 1), which is the modern equivalent of `init`.
- `systemd` spawned the `bash` shell (PID 2100).
- The shell ran the `fork_demo` program (PID 4511 — the parent).
- The parent process then `fork()`-ed, creating the child (PID 4512).
- The depth of the child from `init` is 3 (systemd → bash → fork_demo → fork_demo).

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Process Tree Depth

Let $d(P)$ denote the depth of process $P$ from `init`. Then:

$$d(\text{init}) = 0$$

For any process $P$ created by `fork()` from a parent $Q$:

$$d(P) = d(Q) + 1$$

Therefore, for our example:
- $d(\text{systemd}/1) = 0$
- $d(\text{bash}/2100) = d(\text{systemd}) + 1 = 1$
- $d(\text{fork\_demo}/4511) = d(\text{bash}) + 1 = 2$
- $d(\text{fork\_demo}/4512) = d(\text{fork\_demo}/4511) + 1 = 3$

The general depth formula for a child at the $n$-th generation from `init` is:

$$d_n = d_{n-1} + 1 = d_0 + n$$

Where $d_0 = 0$ (the depth of `init` itself). Thus:

$$d_n = n$$

## 3.7 Error Condition Analysis

| Condition | `fork()` Return | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Normal | $\geq 0$ | Proceed with logic |
| Resource exhausted (PID limit) | $-1$ | `errno = EAGAIN` |
| Memory allocation failure | $-1$ | `errno = ENOMEM` |
| Permissions issue | $-1$ | `errno = EPERM` (rare for fork) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always include the error-checking block. Examiners award marks for proper use of `perror()` or `fprintf(stderr, ...)` when `fork()` fails.

## 3.8 The `wait()` System Call — Synchronization

To prevent **zombie processes** (a child that has finished but whose exit status has not been reaped), the parent should call `wait()`:

$$\text{Parent} \xrightarrow{\text{fork()}} \text{Child} \rightarrow \text{execute} \rightarrow \text{exit} \rightarrow \text{Zombie (if parent doesn't wait)}$$

$$\text{Parent} \xrightarrow{\text{wait(NULL)}} \text{reaps child} \rightarrow \text{Child removed from process table}$$

The signature is:

$$\text{pid\_t\ wait(int *status)}$$

It **blocks** the parent until any of its children terminate, then returns the **PID of the terminated child**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the purpose of the `fork()` system call? Differentiate between the values returned by `fork()` in the parent and child processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `fork()` system call is used to create a new process that is a duplicate of the calling process. The new process is called the **child**, and the original is the **parent**.

**Differentiation of return values:**

| Return Value | Process | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `< 0` | Parent only | Fork failed (e.g., resource limit), no child created |
| `= 0` | Child only | The newly created child process is running this branch |
| `> 0` | Parent only | Successful fork; the value is the **PID of the new child** |

(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the return value table)

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Write a short note on the `pstree` command. How do you use it to view the process tree starting from `init` for a specific process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`pstree` is a Unix command that displays the running processes in a tree format, showing the parent-child relationships between processes. Each process appears as a node, connected to its parent by lines.

To view the process tree of a specific process (say, PID 4512) starting from `init`:

**Step 1:** Run the program and note the PID of the process (e.g., 4512).

**Step 2:** Use either of the following commands:

- `pstree -s 4512` → shows only the **ancestor chain** of PID 4512 up to `init`.
- `pstree -p 4512` → shows the **entire subtree** rooted at PID 4512 with PIDs visible.

The `-p` flag is essential for KTU lab records as it shows numeric PIDs.

(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for the command, 1 for the options)
