> 1. **Forgetting to update $Work$ after each selection** — results in a logically inconsistent safe sequence. This is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using $\lt$ instead of $\le$** in the eligibility test $R_i \le Work$ — a *single-character* error that invalidates the entire answer.
> 3. **Not validating the mix** using F4 — partial-mark cutoff if the problem data is internally inconsistent.
> 4. **Confusing $Max$ with $R$** — if both matrices are given, use $R$ for the detection algorithm and $Max$ for Banker's. Mixing them up forfeits 4 marks.
> 5. **Not printing $Finish$ flags** at the end — the examiner cannot verify the safe sequence without this.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm + Application

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *State and explain the Safety Algorithm used to determine whether a system is in a deadlocked state. Clearly define the data structures $Work$, $Finish$, $Allocation$, and $Request$ used by the algorithm.*

**Model Solution:**

The Safety Algorithm is a deterministic procedure that decides, in polynomial time, whether a given process mix can be scheduled to completion.

**Data structures:**
- $Work[j]$, $j = 1 \dots m$ — a working vector, initialised to $Avail$.
- $Finish[i]$, $i = 1 \dots n$ — a boolean array, initialised to $\text{false}$.
- $Allocation[i][j]$ — the matrix of currently held resources.
- $Request[i][j]$ — the matrix of outstanding resource demands.

**Algorithm steps:**
1. $Work \leftarrow Avail$.
2. $Finish[i] \leftarrow \text{false}, \forall i$.
3. Find an $i$ such that $Finish[i] = \text{false}$ and $Request[i] \le Work$ (component-wise).
4. If such $i$ exists: $Work \leftarrow Work + Allocation[i]$; $Finish[i] \leftarrow \text{true}$; goto Step 3.
5. If no such $i$ exists, terminate. If $Finish[i] = \text{true}, \forall i$, the system is **safe**; otherwise, it is **deadlocked**.

The complexity is $O(m \cdot n^2)$. The greedy approach is provably sufficient because the *Work* vector monotonically increases — once a process is "completed" in simulation, it never needs to be reconsidered.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Consider 5 processes $P_0 \dots P_4$ and 3 resource types $A, B, C$ with $E = \langle 7, 2, 6 \rangle$. The current state is:*
- $Allocation = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, $Request = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, $Avail = \langle 0, 0, 0 \rangle$.
*Is the system deadlocked? Justify using the Safety Algorithm.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (Validation):**
Check F4 for each column.
- Col A: $0+2+3+2+0 + 0 = 7 = E_A$. ✓
- Col B: $1+0+0+1+0 + 0 = 2 = E_B$. ✓
- Col C: $0+0+3+1+2 + 0 = 6 = E_C$. ✓

The mix is valid.

**Step 2 (Safety Algorithm Execution):**

Initialise: $Work = \langle 0, 0, 0 \rangle$, $Finish = \langle F, F, F, F, F \rangle$.

**Iteration 1:** Scan for a process with $Request[i] \le Work = \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$.
- $P_0$: $\langle 4,0,2 \rangle \not\le \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$ (4 > 0). ✗
- $P_1$: $\langle 1,0,0 \rangle \not\le \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$ (1 > 0). ✗
- $P_2$: $\langle 0,0,1 \rangle \not\le \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$ (1 > 0). ✗
- $P_3$: $\langle 1,1,0 \rangle \not\le \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$. ✗
- $P_4$: $\langle 2,0,2 \rangle \not\le \langle 0,0,0 \rangle$. ✗

No process can be selected. The algorithm halts with $Finish = \langle F, F, F, F, F \rangle$.

**Conclusion:** Since at least one $Finish[i]$ is false, **the system is in a DEADLOCKED state**, and *all* processes $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$ are deadlocked. No safe sequence exists.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + Comparative

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the difference between Deadlock Prevention, Avoidance, and Detection. In which of these categories does the "obtain process mix and determine if deadlocked" experiment fall? Justify.*

**Model Solution:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | When Applied | Drawback |
|:---------|:----------|:-------------|:---------|
| **Prevention** | Statically negates one of the four Coffman conditions (e.g., impose total ordering on resources to break circular wait). | At system design time. | Conservative — under-utilises resources. |
| **Avoidance** | Dynamically uses a priori information (e.g., $Max$ matrix) to never enter an unsafe state. **Banker's Algorithm** is the canonical example. | At every resource request. | Requires *future maximum demand*; unrealistic in practice. |
| **Detection** | Lets deadlock occur; runs an algorithm periodically to detect it, then *recovers* (abort a process, preempt a resource, or roll back). | At runtime, periodically or on-demand. | Recovery cost; possible loss of work. |

The lab experiment "**obtain a process mix and determine if the system is deadlocked**" falls squarely under **Detection**. The procedure does not prevent or avoid — it *observes* a current state and *classifies* it as safe or deadlocked, leaving any recovery action to a subsequent module (e.g., Module 3 on process scheduling with rollback).

**(b) [7 Marks]** *With the same data as Question A(b), suppose a new instance of resource $A$ is added to the system (i.e., $Avail = \langle 1, 0, 0 \rangle$). Re-run the Safety Algorithm and report the new safe sequence, if any.*

**Model Solution:**

**Initialisation:** $Work = \langle 1, 0, 0 \rangle$, $Finish = \langle F, F, F, F, F \rangle$.

**Iteration 1:** Scan for $Request[i] \le Work = \langle 1,0,0 \rangle$.
- $P_0$: $\langle 4,0,2 \rangle$ — ✗
- $P_1$: $\langle 1,0,0 \rangle$ — ✓ **SELECT $P_1$**

Update: $Work = \langle 1,0,0 \rangle + \langle 2,0,0 \rangle = \langle 3,0,0 \rangle$; $Finish = \langle F, T, F, F, F \rangle$.

**Iteration 2:** Scan for $Request[i] \le \langle 3,0,0 \rangle$.
- $P_0$: $\langle 4,0,2 \rangle$ — ✗
- $P_2$: $\langle 0,0,1 \rangle$ — ✗ (1 > 0)
- $P_3$: $\langle 1,1,0 \rangle$ — ✗ (1 > 0)
- $P_4$: $\langle 2,0,2 \rangle$ — ✗ (2 > 0)

No process selectable! The algorithm halts.

**Conclusion:** Even with one extra instance of $A$, the system is **still deadlocked** because processes $P_2, P_4$ are blocked waiting for resource $C$ and $B$ respectively, while holding $A$. No safe sequence exists.

> *Examiner's Note:* This part rewards students who **do not blindly accept "one more resource = safe"**. The structural cycle is the binding constraint, not raw resource count.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A *process mix* = $\{Allocation, Request, Available\}$ snapshot of a system with $n$ processes and $m$ resource types.
- **Validity Check (F4):** $\sum_i A_{ij} + Avail_j = E_j$ for every $j$ — always run this first.
- **Safety Algorithm:** Two arrays $Work$ and $Finish$; iterate until no process satisfies $R_i \le Work$ (component-wise $\le$).
- **Safe vs. Unsafe:** Safe $\iff$ a safe sequence exists $\iff$ a path to completion exists. Unsafe $\not\Rightarrow$ deadlocked *immediately*, but $\Rightarrow$ deadlock is *possible*.
- **Greedy Choice:** The algorithm picks *any* eligible process — it is provably correct and complete.
- **Complexity:** $O(m \cdot n^2)$ in the worst case — the outer loop runs $\le n$ times, the inner scan is $O(m \cdot n)$.
- **Output Format:** Always report (i) the safe sequence (if any), (ii) the final $Finish$ vector, (iii) the set of deadlocked processes (if any).
- **Resource Allocation Graph:** A cycle in the RAG is **necessary but not sufficient** for deadlock (sufficient only when each resource type has *exactly one* instance).
- **Confusion Traps:** $Max$ vs. $Request$; $<$ vs. $\le$; forgetting to add $Allocation[i]$ to $Work$; ignoring the validation step.
- **Real-world link:** The algorithm is the exact foundation of `deadlock_detection()` in the Linux kernel's lock validator and Java's `ThreadMXBean`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Validate the mix, initialise $Work$ and $Finish$, scan-and-select repeatedly, and always print the safe sequence or the deadlocked set.* This is the **four-step ritual** that guarantees full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and lab viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement the deadlock-free semaphore-based solution for the dining philosopher's problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL407 - OPERATING SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement the deadlock-free semaphore-based solution for the dining philosopher's problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dining Philosophers Problem (Dijkstra, 1965)**
> A classical **synchronization problem** in operating systems that illustrates the challenges of **resource contention**, **deadlock**, and **starvation** when multiple concurrent processes compete for a finite set of shared resources.

The problem describes **5 philosophers** seated around a circular table. Between every pair of adjacent philosophers lies **one chopstick** (or fork). A philosopher alternates between two states — **THINKING** and **EATING**. To eat, a philosopher must acquire **both** the left and right chopsticks. A philosopher can put down a chopstick only after finishing eating.

The objective is to design a **deadlock-free** and **starvation-free (as far as practical)** concurrent algorithm that allows maximum parallelism using **binary semaphores** and **mutex locks**.

## 1.2 The Five Formal States of a Philosopher

| State | Code | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **THINKING** | `0` | Philosopher is not attempting to eat; doing CPU work. |
| **HUNGRY** | `1` | Philosopher wants to eat; waiting for both chopsticks. |
| **EATING** | `2` | Philosopher has both chopsticks and is eating. |

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Office Pantry**
> Imagine **5 developers** sitting around a small table. Between every two developers is **one pen**. To write code on paper, a developer needs **both** the pen on their left and the pen on their right. If all 5 developers simultaneously pick up their left pen, **no one can pick up the right pen** — the system **deadlocks**. The problem asks: how do we coordinate so everyone gets a fair chance to write without anyone starving forever?

This problem is the canonical illustration of the **Four Coffman Conditions for Deadlock**:
1. **Mutual Exclusion** — Only one philosopher can hold a chopstick at a time.
2. **Hold and Wait** — A philosopher holds one chopstick while waiting for the other.
3. **No Preemption** — A chopstick cannot be forcibly taken from a philosopher.
4. **Circular Wait** — A cycle of philosophers wait for each other's chopsticks.

The solution presented here **breaks Circular Wait** by introducing a **centralized state machine** guarded by a mutex.

## 1.4 System Constants Used

- **N = 5** (Number of philosophers and chopsticks)
- **THINKING_TIME** — Arbitrary delay (used in simulation)
- **EAT_TIME** — Arbitrary delay (used in simulation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Naive (Deadlock-Prone) Solution

The simplest solution uses an array of **5 binary semaphores**, one per chopstick:

```c
sem_t chopstick[5] = {1, 1, 1, 1, 1};
```

A philosopher `i` picks up left then right:

```c
wait(&chopstick[i]);         // Pick left
wait(&chopstick[(i+1)%5]);   // Pick right
/* EAT */
signal(&chopstick[i]);
signal(&chopstick[(i+1)%5]);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **DEADLOCK GUARANTEED!** If all 5 philosophers pick up their left chopstick simultaneously, a circular wait forms: P0→P1→P2→P3→P4→P0. **No philosopher can ever eat.** This is exactly the failure mode our solution must prevent.

## 2.2 Why the Galvin Solution is Deadlock-Free

The deadlock-free semaphore solution (popularized by **Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne**) works on a fundamentally different principle. Instead of guarding individual chopsticks, it guards the **state transition** of each philosopher through a **centralized mutex** and an array of **private semaphores** (one per philosopher).

### 2.2.1 The Three Data Structures

1. **`state[N]`** — Global array tracking each philosopher's state (THINKING / HUNGRY / EATING).
2. **`mutex` (binary semaphore)** — Ensures only **one philosopher at a time** can examine or modify `state[]`. Initialized to **1**.
3. **`self[N]` (one binary semaphore per philosopher)** — Acts as a **private blocking queue** for each philosopher. Initialized to **0**. When a philosopher becomes EATING, `signal(&self[i])` wakes them up.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — `philosopher(i)`** runs an infinite loop: `think() → take_forks(i) → eat() → put_forks(i)`.

**Step 2 — `take_forks(i)`**:
- Acquires the **global mutex** (only one philosopher can test the table state at a time).
- Sets `state[i] = HUNGRY`.
- Calls `test(i)` — a function that checks if both neighbors are NOT EATING. If so, transitions `state[i] = EATING` and signals `self[i]`.
- Releases the mutex.
- **Blocks** on `wait(&self[i])` if `test()` did not grant permission.

**Step 3 — `test(i)`** (the heart of the deadlock-free guarantee):
- Checks `state[i] == HUNGRY AND state[LEFT] != EATING AND state[RIGHT] != EATING`.
- If true, sets `state[i] = EATING` and `signal(&self[i])`.
- **Crucially**, a philosopher never *holds* a chopstick while waiting. The right to eat is granted **atomically** after both neighbors are confirmed safe.

**Step 4 — `put_forks(i)`**:
- Acquires mutex, sets `state[i] = THINKING`.
- Re-tests **both** neighbors (LEFT and RIGHT) — if either is HUNGRY and now both neighbors are free, they are granted the EATING state.
- Releases mutex.

### 2.3 Why Deadlock is Impossible

| Coffman Condition | How the Solution Breaks It |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mutual Exclusion | Still exists (binary semaphores). |
| Hold and Wait | **BROKEN** — A philosopher never holds a chopstick while waiting. Permission to eat is granted only when both neighbors are not eating. |
| No Preemption | Still exists. |
| Circular Wait | **BROKEN** — The `test()` function is the single arbiter. A cycle can never form because state transitions are serialized by `mutex`. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Value | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Number of Philosophers | $N = 5$ | Fixed by the problem. |
| Left Neighbor of $i$ | $(i + N - 1) \bmod N$ | Index of left chopstick. |
| Right Neighbor of $i$ | $(i + 1) \bmod N$ | Index of right chopstick. |
| Mutex Initial Value | $1$ | Binary semaphore for state array protection. |
| `self[i]` Initial Value | $0$ | Each philosopher blocks on their own semaphore. |
| `state[i]` Domain | $\{0, 1, 2\}$ | THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING. |
| Test Condition | $\text{state}[i] = \text{HUNGRY} \land \text{state}[L] \neq \text{EATING} \land \text{state}[R] \neq \text{EATING}$ | All-or-nothing transition to EATING. |
| Max Concurrent Eaters | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor = 2$ | Theoretical max (no two adjacent eat). |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem directly models **multi-threaded resource allocators** in real systems:
> - **Database connection pools** with N connections and N queries needing 2 each.
> - **I/O device drivers** where a process needs 2 of 5 DMA channels.
> - **Distributed lock managers** in clustered file systems.
> - **Producer-Consumer** variants in message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ).

The `test()`-and-`state[]` pattern is the conceptual ancestor of **Linux's wait queues** and **Windows dispatcher objects**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Algorithm, Derivations & Complete C Implementation

## 3.1 Derivation of the Test Condition

We need to formally prove when a philosopher $i$ may safely transition to EATING.

**Step 1 — Define the safety condition:**
A philosopher $i$ may enter EATING state if and only if both adjacent philosophers ($L$ and $R$) are not EATING.

$$\text{SAFE}(i) \iff \left( \text{state}[L] \neq \text{EATING} \right) \land \left( \text{state}[R] \neq \text{EATING} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Add the precondition that $i$ is interested:**
The philosopher must be in the HUNGRY state (actively requesting).

$$\text{GRANT}(i) \iff \text{state}[i] = \text{HUNGRY} \land \text{SAFE}(i)$$

**Step 3 — Show that circular wait is impossible:**

Suppose, for contradiction, a circular wait forms. Then each philosopher $P_k$ in the cycle holds the **right to eat** granted by `test()`. By `test()`'s logic, each granted philosopher has both neighbors not EATING. But for a cycle of length 5, this is impossible — at most $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor = 2$ non-adjacent philosophers can simultaneously satisfy the test.

$$\therefore \text{ Deadlock is structurally impossible. } \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Starvation analysis:**

While deadlock is impossible, **starvation is technically possible** in the Galvin solution — a philosopher whose neighbors are perpetually hungry may never get a chance. To guarantee starvation-freedom, one must add a **FIFO queue** discipline. KTU questions usually accept the solution as **"deadlock-free"**; note this caveat in viva.

## 3.2 Full Algorithm in Pseudocode (Galvin Style)

```
PHILOSOPHER(i):
    while TRUE:
        think()
        take_forks(i)
        eat()
        put_forks(i)

TAKE_FORKS(i):
    wait(mutex)
    state[i] = HUNGRY
    test(i)
    signal(mutex)
    wait(self[i])

PUT_FORKS(i):
    wait(mutex)
    state[i] = THINKING
    test(LEFT)
    test(RIGHT)
    signal(mutex)

TEST(i):
    if state[i] == HUNGRY and
       state[LEFT]  != EATING and
       state[RIGHT] != EATING:
        state[i] = EATING
        signal(self[i])
```

## 3.3 Complete C Implementation (POSIX Semaphores)

This is the **executable program** you submit for the KTU lab record.

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pthread.h>
#include <semaphore.h>

#define N 5
#define LEFT  (i + N - 1) % N
#define RIGHT (i + 1) % N

#define THINKING 0
#define HUNGRY   1
#define EATING   2

/* ---------- Global Synchronization Primitives ---------- */
sem_t mutex;          /* Protects state[] */
sem_t self[N];        /* One private semaphore per philosopher */
int   state[N];       /* State of each philosopher */

/* ---------- Forward Declarations ---------- */
void *philosopher(void *num);
void  take_forks(int i);
void  put_forks(int i);
void  test(int i);
void  think(int i);
void  eat(int i);

/* ---------- Main ---------- */
int main(void) {
    int i;
    int phil[N] = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4};
    pthread_t thread_id[N];

    sem_init(&mutex, 0, 1);
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        sem_init(&self[i], 0, 0);
        state[i] = THINKING;
    }

    for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
        pthread_create(&thread_id[i], NULL, philosopher, &phil[i]);

    for (i = 0; i < N; i++)
        pthread_join(thread_id[i], NULL);

    return 0;
}

/* ---------- Philosopher Thread ---------- */
void *philosopher(void *num) {
    int i = *((int *)num);
    while (1) {
        think(i);
        take_forks(i);
        eat(i);
        put_forks(i);
    }
}

/* ---------- Try to Acquire Both Chopsticks ---------- */
void take_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);
    state[i] = HUNGRY;
    printf("Philosopher %d is HUNGRY\n", i);
    test(i);
    sem_post(&mutex);
    sem_wait(&self[i]);          /* Block if not granted EATING */
}

/* ---------- Release Both Chopsticks ---------- */
void put_forks(int i) {
    sem_wait(&mutex);
    state[i] = THINKING;
    printf("Philosopher %d puts down chopsticks and starts THINKING\n", i);
    test(LEFT);
    test(RIGHT);
    sem_post(&mutex);
}

/* ---------- The Deadlock-Free Decision Function ---------- */
void test(int i) {
    if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
        state[LEFT]  != EATING &&
        state[RIGHT] != EATING) {
        state[i] = EATING;
        printf("Philosopher %d starts EATING\n", i);
        sem_post(&self[i]);      /* Wake up the calling philosopher */
    }
}

/* ---------- Simulated Activities ---------- */
void think(int i) {
    printf("Philosopher %d is THINKING\n", i);
    sleep(rand() % 3 + 1);
}

void eat(int i) {
    printf("Philosopher %d is EATING\n", i);
    sleep(rand() % 3 + 1);
}
```

## 3.4 Compilation & Execution Steps (Linux / WSL / Ubuntu)

```bash
# Step 1 — Save the program
gedit dining.c

# Step 2 — Compile with pthread support
gcc dining.c -o dining -lpthread

# Step 3 — Run
./dining
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Press Ctrl+C to terminate** the infinite loops, or insert a finite count and `break` inside `philosopher()`.

## 3.5 Sample Output (Truncated)

```
Philosopher 0 is THINKING
Philosopher 1 is THINKING
Philosopher 2 is THINKING
Philosopher 3 is THINKING
Philosopher 4 is THINKING
Philosopher 2 is HUNGRY
Philosopher 2 starts EATING
Philosopher 2 is EATING
Philosopher 2 puts down chopsticks and starts THINKING
Philosopher 2 is THINKING
...
```

Notice: **At no point do all 5 philosophers enter HUNGRY simultaneously and block forever** — the test() function with the mutex guarantees this.

## 3.6 Alternative Deadlock-Free Solution: Semaphore Limit (4 Philosophers)

A simpler (though less concurrent) approach restricts the table to **N-1 = 4 philosophers** using a counting semaphore.

```c
sem_t room;            /* Counting semaphore, init = N - 1 = 4 */

void philosopher(int i) {
    while (1) {
        think();
        sem_wait(&room);       /* Enter room (max 4 inside) */
        wait(&chopstick[i]);
        wait(&chopstick[(i+1)%N]);
        eat();
        signal(&chopstick[i]);
        signal(&chopstick[(i+1)%N]);
        sem_post(&room);       /* Leave room */
    }
}
```

**Why this works:** With only 4 philosophers inside, at least one philosopher will be unable to pick up a left chopstick — breaking the circular wait condition. This solution is **conceptually easier to explain** and often appears in KTU Part A questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Dining Philosophers Problem. What are the three possible states of a philosopher?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Dining Philosophers Problem** was proposed by **Dijkstra (1965)** to illustrate the challenges of **process synchronization** and **deadlock** in concurrent systems. It involves **5 philosophers** seated around a circular table with **5 chopsticks** placed between them. To eat, a philosopher needs both the left and right chopsticks. A philosopher spends time in two main activities — **thinking** and **eating** — and alternates between them.

The three possible states of a philosopher are:
1. **THINKING** — Not attempting to eat; the philosopher is using the CPU.
2. **HUNGRY** — Wants to eat and is trying to acquire both chopsticks.
3. **EATING** — Has acquired both chopsticks and is currently eating.

The challenge is to design a synchronization protocol that **prevents deadlock** and **maximizes concurrency** while allowing all philosophers to eventually eat. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between deadlock and starvation in the context of the dining philosophers problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deadlock | Starvation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | All (or a subset of) philosophers are blocked forever, waiting for chopsticks held by others. | A specific philosopher waits indefinitely even though the system as a whole is making progress. |
| **Scope** | System-wide freeze; no philosopher in the deadlocked set can proceed. | Localized; other philosophers continue to eat. |
| **Cause** | Circular wait between processes. | Unfair scheduling — a philosopher is perpetually bypassed. |
| **Example** | All 5 philosophers pick up their left chopstick simultaneously. | Philosopher 2 is always preempted by hungry neighbors 1 and 3. |
| **Solution** | Break the circular wait condition (e.g., Galvin test() function). | Implement a fair queue (FIFO) for `self[i]` semaphores. |

The Galvin semaphore solution **prevents deadlock** but does **not strictly guarantee starvation-freedom** without additional queuing discipline. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the **LEFT** and **RIGHT** chopstick indices. The KTU convention is $\text{LEFT} = (i + N - 1) \bmod N$ and $\text{RIGHT} = (i + 1) \bmod N$. Getting this wrong in the code costs **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to initialize `self[i] = 0` results in philosophers not blocking properly — a **silent logical bug** the examiner will deduct marks for.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **Galvin solution** (uses `state[]` and `test()`) with the **simple chopstick semaphore solution** (deadlock-prone). Read the question carefully — "deadlock-free" specifies the Galvin approach.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not explaining **why** the solution is deadlock-free (must reference the **broken circular wait** Coffman condition) — loses **2-3 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Galvin's Deadlock-Free Solution

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of suitable data structures, explain the **deadlock-free semaphore-based solution** for the dining philosophers problem. State the data structures used and their initial values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete C program using **POSIX semaphores** and **pthreads** to implement the above solution. Explain how the `test()` function prevents deadlock. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Data Structures and Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

The deadlock-free solution uses the following data structures:

**1. State Array — `state[N]`** where $N = 5$:
- An integer array tracking the current state of each philosopher.
- Valid values: `THINKING (0)`, `HUNGRY (1)`, `EATING (2)`.
- Initially: `state[i] = THINKING` for all $i$.

**2. Mutex Semaphore — `mutex`:**
- A **binary semaphore** initialized to **1**.
- Ensures that only **one philosopher at a time** can inspect or modify the `state[]` array.
- Protects the critical section where state transitions occur.

**3. Private Semaphores — `self[N]`:**
- An array of **5 binary semaphores**, one per philosopher.
- Initialized to **0** (i.e., each philosopher starts in a blocked state).
- When philosopher $i$ is granted permission to eat, `signal(&self[i])` is called to unblock them.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Philosopher $i$'s main loop:** `think() → take_forks(i) → eat() → put_forks(i)`.

2. **`take_forks(i)`:**
   - `wait(&mutex)` — enter critical section.
   - `state[i] = HUNGRY` — declare intent to eat.
   - `test(i)` — check if both neighbors are not eating.
   - `signal(&mutex)` — exit critical section.
   - `wait(&self[i])` — **block** if not granted EATING permission.

3. **`put_forks(i)`:**
   - `wait(&mutex)` — enter critical section.
   - `state[i] = THINKING` — finished eating.
   - Call `test(LEFT)` and `test(RIGHT)` — give waiting neighbors a chance.
   - `signal(&mutex)` — exit critical section.

4. **`test(i)` — the heart of the algorithm:**
   - Condition: `state[i] == HUNGRY AND state[LEFT] != EATING AND state[RIGHT] != EATING`.
   - If true, set `state[i] = EATING` and `signal(&self[i])`.

**Why it is deadlock-free:** The `test()` function is the **central arbiter**. A philosopher **never holds a chopstick while waiting**. The right to eat is granted **only when both neighbors are not eating**, and this check is performed **atomically** under the protection of `mutex`. This **breaks the circular wait** condition, which is one of the four Coffman conditions necessary for deadlock. Therefore, deadlock is structurally impossible. **(7 marks — full distribution: 2 marks for data structures, 3 marks for algorithm steps, 2 marks for deadlock analysis)**

---

**(b) C Program Implementation (7 marks):**

The complete C program is given in **Section 3.3** of these notes. The student should reproduce it with the following key elements:

| Component | Marks |
| :--- | :---: |
| Correct `#include` directives and `#define` macros (`N`, `LEFT`, `RIGHT`, state constants) | 1 |
| Correct initialization of `mutex` (=1) and `self[N]` (=0) in `main()` | 1 |
| Correct `philosopher()` thread function with infinite loop | 1 |
| Correct `take_forks()` with mutex acquire, `state[i]=HUNGRY`, `test(i)`, mutex release, `wait(&self[i])` | 1.5 |
| Correct `put_forks()` with mutex acquire, `state[i]=THINKING`, `test(LEFT)`, `test(RIGHT)`, mutex release | 1.5 |
| Correct `test()` function with the three-way condition check and `signal(&self[i])` | 1 |

**Explanation of `test()` preventing deadlock:**

The `test()` function is called **only inside a `wait(&mutex)` critical section**. This means the condition `state[LEFT] != EATING AND state[RIGHT] != EATING` is evaluated **atomically**. Even if multiple philosophers are HUNGRY, only one `test()` call runs at a time. The function grants EATING status to **at most one** of the eligible philosophers per critical section. Since a philosopher **does not hold any resource** before the EATING state is granted, the **hold-and-wait** condition is broken, and consequently, **circular wait cannot form**. Thus, deadlock is impossible by construction. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Approach & Analysis

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **naive semaphore solution** for the dining philosophers problem and show with a **resource allocation graph** or step-by-step trace why it leads to deadlock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Propose a **modified solution using a counting semaphore** that limits the number of philosophers at the table to **N-1**. Show the modified `philosopher()` function and explain why this prevents deadlock. Mention any one disadvantage of this approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Naive Solution and Deadlock Demonstration (7 marks):**

**Naive Solution:**

```c
sem_t chopstick[5] = {1, 1, 1, 1, 1};

void philosopher(int i) {
    while (1) {
        think();
        wait(&chopstick[i]);          // Pick LEFT
        wait(&chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);  // Pick RIGHT
        eat();
        signal(&chopstick[i]);
        signal(&chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step deadlock trace** (initial state: all chopsticks free, all philosophers THINKING):

| Time | P0 | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| t1 | Picks C0 | Picks C1 | Picks C2 | Picks C3 | Picks C4 |
| t2 | Tries C4 → **BLOCKED** | Tries C0 → **BLOCKED** | Tries C3 → **BLOCKED** | Tries C2 → **BLOCKED** | Tries C1 → **BLOCKED** |

**Result:** All 5 philosophers are blocked, each holding exactly one chopstick and waiting for another. The system is **permanently deadlocked**.

**Why deadlock occurs:** All four Coffman conditions are satisfied:
- *Mutual Exclusion:* Each chopstick held by exactly one philosopher.
- *Hold and Wait:* Each philosopher holds one chopstick while waiting for another.
- *No Preemption:* A chopstick cannot be forcibly taken.
- *Circular Wait:* P0 → C4 → P4 → C1 → P1 → C0 → P0. **(7 marks — 3 for code, 2 for trace table, 2 for Coffman analysis)**

---

**(b) Modified Solution with Counting Semaphore (7 marks):**

We introduce a **counting semaphore `room`** initialized to **N-1 = 4**. A philosopher must acquire a `room` token before attempting to pick up chopsticks.

**Modified Code:**

```c
sem_t chopstick[5] = {1, 1, 1, 1, 1};
sem_t room;                    /* Counting semaphore */

void init() {
    sem_init(&room, 0, 4);     /* Allow at most 4 inside */
}

void philosopher(int i) {
    while (1) {
        think();
        sem_wait(&room);                   /* Enter the room (4 max) */
        sem_wait(&chopstick[i]);
        sem_wait(&chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
        eat();
        sem_post(&chopstick[i]);
        sem_post(&chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
        sem_post(&room);                   /* Leave the room */
    }
}
```

**Why it prevents deadlock:**

With at most **4 philosophers** in the room at any time, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least one philosopher will be unable to enter. The 4 philosophers inside may all try to pick up their left chopstick, but the 5th philosopher (outside) is excluded. The 4 inside will eventually have a configuration where circular wait cannot close — for example, if P0, P1, P2, P3 each hold their left chopstick, then P3's right chopstick is C4, which is **not held by anyone** (P4 is outside). So P3 can complete its `wait(&chopstick[(3+1)%5]) = wait(&chopstick[0])`... actually P0 holds C0. Hmm.

**Correction:** With 4 inside, the worst case is each holds their left chopstick, leaving 1 chopstick unheld. That unheld chopstick is the right chopstick of one of the 4 inside, breaking the cycle. Hence, circular wait is impossible.

**Disadvantage:** **Reduced concurrency** — at most 4 philosophers can be in the "thinking about eating" phase; if N=5, one philosopher is always excluded. This wastes potential parallelism. **(7 marks — 2 for code, 3 for proof, 2 for disadvantage)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- [ ] **Dining Philosophers Problem** was proposed by **Edsger Dijkstra in 1965**.
- [ ] **N = 5** philosophers and **5 chopsticks** in the classical formulation.
- [ ] The three states are **THINKING (0), HUNGRY (1), EATING (2)**.
- [ ] The **naive solution** (one semaphore per chopstick) is **deadlock-prone**.
- [ ] The **Galvin deadlock-free solution** uses **3 data structures**: `state[]`, `mutex`, and `self[]`.
- [ ] **Mutex** is initialized to **1**; **`self[i]`** is initialized to **0** for all $i$.
- [ ] **LEFT neighbor** of $i$ is $(i + N - 1) \bmod N$; **RIGHT neighbor** is $(i + 1) \bmod N$.
- [ ] The **`test(i)` function** is the deadlock-breaking arbiter — it checks if both neighbors are not EATING.
- [ ] A philosopher **never holds a chopstick while waiting** — permission to eat is granted **atomically**.
- [ ] The solution **breaks the circular wait** Coffman condition.
- [ ] **Deadlock is impossible**; **starvation is technically possible** without FIFO queuing.
- [ ] The **alternative limit-based solution** uses a counting semaphore `room` initialized to **N-1 = 4**.
- [ ] The **four Coffman conditions** are: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
- [ ] **Maximum simultaneous eaters** = $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor = 2$ (no two adjacent can eat).
- [ ] For KTU lab record, you must include: **Aim, Algorithm, Program, Output, Result, Viva Questions**.
- [ ] **Common viva questions:** Why use a mutex and not a binary semaphore per chopstick? What happens if `self[i]` is initialized to 1? What is the difference between deadlock and starvation?

The output should be the physical address corresponding to the virtual address in <frame number, offset> format. You may assume that the page table is implemented as an array indexed by page numbers. (NB: If the page table has no index for the page number determined from the virtual address, you may just declare a page table miss)

---

# Module Experiment 2: Paging – Address Translation Simulation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paging** is a non-contiguous memory management scheme that eliminates external fragmentation by dividing the process's **logical (virtual) address space** into fixed-size blocks called **pages** and the **physical memory (RAM)** into fixed-size blocks called **frames** (or page frames). A hardware-supported data structure called the **Page Table** maintains the mapping from virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers, enabling the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** to perform address translation at runtime.

For this experiment, the **virtual address** is a single integer (in decimal) issued by the CPU. The job of the program is to:

1. **Decode** the virtual address into a *(page number, offset)* pair.
2. **Consult** a page table (an array indexed by page number) to fetch the corresponding *frame number*.
3. **Reconstruct** the physical location as `<frame number, offset>`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary**
> - **Virtual Address Space (VAS):** The maximum logical address range a process *thinks* it owns. Given in **MB**.
> - **Page:** A fixed-size block of the virtual address space. Size given in **KB**.
> - **Page Number (p):** The index into the page table; identifies *which* page of the process is being accessed.
> - **Offset (d):** The displacement *within* the page; specifies the exact byte.
> - **Frame Number (f):** The index in physical RAM; output of the page table lookup.
> - **Page Table:** An array `PT[p] = f` (or an invalid marker if the page is not resident).
> - **Page Fault / Page Table Miss:** A page table entry indicates the page is not currently mapped in RAM (e.g., `PT[p] == -1`).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Staircase" Model

Imagine a huge library (the **virtual address space**) where every book has a unique *call number* (the **virtual address**). But the building's actual shelves (the **physical memory**) are far smaller and scattered across different rooms.

- The librarian divides the entire collection into **uniform cardboard boxes** (the **pages**), each holding, say, 4 KB of books.
- Each box is labeled with a **box number** (the **page number**).
- The librarian keeps a **register** (the **page table**) that says: *"Box 17 is currently sitting on Shelf Room 3, Bay 5"* (the **frame number**).
- Inside a box, every book has a **slot index** (the **offset**).

When you (the CPU) ask for call number `v`:
1. The librarian divides `v` by the box capacity to find *(box, slot)*.
2. The register is consulted to convert the box number to a room-bay location (the frame).
3. The final answer is given as `<room-bay, slot>`.

This entire lookup must be done **mathematically with bit-shifts and array indexing** — which is exactly what our C program simulates.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Experiment

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Virtual address space size | **MB** ($2^{20}$ bytes) |
| $P$ | Page size | **KB** ($2^{10}$ bytes) |
| $N_{pages}$ | Total number of pages in the VAS | dimensionless |
| $VA$ | Virtual address (input, decimal) | bytes |
| $p$ | Page number | dimensionless index |
| $d$ | Offset within the page | bytes |
| $f$ | Frame number (from page table) | dimensionless index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MB and KB?** Because real-world OS textbooks (Silberschatz, Galvin) and the KTU question paper always use these powers-of-two units. $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20}$ bytes and $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10}$ bytes. This gives *clean* bit-shifts when page sizes are powers of two — a deliberate choice in OS design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematics of Address Translation

The virtual address is conceptually split into two bit-fields:

$$
VA = \underbrace{p}_{\text{high-order bits}} \;\;\; \Big\vert\;\;\; \underbrace{d}_{\text{low-order bits}}
$$

The boundary between $p$ and $d$ is determined entirely by the **page size $P$** in bytes. If $P$ is a power of two, the split is clean and uses bit-masking; otherwise, we use **integer division and modulo**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** For this experiment, we must support **arbitrary page sizes** (not just powers of two), so the safe, general approach is **integer division and modulo**, not bit-shifts.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Parse command-line arguments** (`argc` should be 4; `argv[1]`, `argv[2]`, `argv[3]`).
2. **Convert** $S$ (MB) to bytes: $S_{bytes} = S \times 2^{20}$.
3. **Convert** $P$ (KB) to bytes: $P_{bytes} = P \times 2^{10}$.
4. **Compute total number of pages** in the VAS:
$$N_{pages} = \frac{S \times 2^{20}}{P \times 2^{10}} = \frac{S}{P} \times 2^{10}$$
5. **Extract page number** $p$ from $VA$:
$$p = \left\lfloor \frac{VA}{P_{bytes}} \right\rfloor$$
6. **Extract offset** $d$ from $VA$:
$$d = VA \bmod P_{bytes}$$
7. **Boundary check** — verify the virtual address lies within the VAS:
$$0 \le VA < S_{bytes} \quad \text{(else "Invalid virtual address")}$$
8. **Boundary check** — verify $p < N_{pages}$ (the page index is valid).
9. **Look up** the page table: $f = PT[p]$.
10. **Handle miss:** if $p$ is beyond the populated page-table entries, declare **"Page table miss"**.
11. **Output:** print `<f, d>`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VAS in bytes | $S_{b} = S \times 2^{20}$ | bytes |
| 2 | Page size in bytes | $P_{b} = P \times 2^{10}$ | bytes |
| 3 | Total pages | $N = S_{b} \,/\, P_{b} = (S / P) \times 2^{10}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Page number | $p = VA \,/\, P_{b}$ (integer division) | index |
| 5 | Offset | $d = VA \bmod P_{b}$ | bytes, $0 \le d < P_{b}$ |
| 6 | Frame number | $f = PT[p]$ | from array lookup |
| 7 | Valid VA range | $0 \le VA < S_{b}$ | bytes |
| 8 | Power-of-two shortcut | $p = VA \gg \log_{2} P_{b}$, $d = VA \,\&\, (P_{b}-1)$ | only if $P_{b}$ is a power of two |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute $N$ as $S / P$ in MB/KB, forgetting the $2^{10}$ factor. Always normalize to **bytes first**, then divide.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Paging and address translation lie at the heart of:
- **Virtual memory systems** (Windows, Linux) — every process gets a private 4 GB (or larger) virtual address space.
- **Demand paging** — only the actively used pages are kept in RAM; the rest live on disk.
- **Memory protection** — the page table can flag read-only, execute-only, kernel-only pages.
- **Cloud and embedded systems** — small IoT devices with paging use small page sizes (4 KB) to minimize internal fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Model Derivation)

Suppose the command line is:
```
./paging 8 4 5000
```
That is, $S = 8$ MB, $P = 4$ KB, $VA = 5000$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
S_{b} = 8 \times 2^{20} = 8 \times 1048576 = 8388608 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
P_{b} = 4 \times 2^{10} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Total number of pages.**

$$
N = \frac{S_{b}}{P_{b}} = \frac{8388608}{4096} = 2048 \text{ pages}
$$

**Step 3 — Page number $p$.**

$$
p = \left\lfloor \frac{5000}{4096} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1.2207\ldots \right\rfloor = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Offset $d$.**

$$
d = 5000 \bmod 4096 = 5000 - 1 \times 4096 = 904
$$

**Step 5 — Validity checks.**
- $0 \le 5000 < 8388608$ ✓ (valid VA)
- $p = 1 < N = 2048$ ✓ (valid page index)

**Step 6 — Page-table lookup.**

Assume the test case pre-fills `PT[1] = 7` (frame 7). Then $f = 7$.

**Step 7 — Output.**

```
Physical address: <7, 904>
```

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Edge Cases

| Input $(S, P, VA)$ | $S_b$ | $P_b$ | $N$ | $p$ | $d$ | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(8, 4, 0)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | 0 | 0 | Page-table entry $\to$ frame |
| $(8, 4, 4095)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | 0 | 4095 | Boundary of page 0 |
| $(8, 4, 4096)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | 1 | 0 | Start of page 1 |
| $(8, 4, 8388607)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | 2047 | 4095 | Last byte of VAS |
| $(8, 4, 8388608)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | 2048 | 0 | **Invalid — out of VAS** |
| $(8, 4, 99999999)$ | 8388608 | 4096 | 2048 | — | — | **Page table miss / invalid VA** |
| $(2, 8, 12345)$ | 2097152 | 8192 | 256 | 1 | 4153 | Larger page size |

---

### 3.3 Full Reference C Program (Compilation-Ready)

```c
/* paging.c
 * Simulate address translation in a paging scheme.
 * Usage : ./paging <VAS_MB> <PageSize_KB> <VirtualAddress_decimal>
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    if (argc != 4) {
        fprintf(stderr,
                "Usage: %s <VAS_MB> <PageSize_KB> <VirtualAddress>\n",
                argv[0]);
        return 1;
    }

    /* ---------- 1. Parse and convert ---------- */
    long long S_MB   = atoll(argv[1]);                 /* VAS in MB        */
    long long P_KB   = atoll(argv[2]);                 /* Page size in KB  */
    long long VA     = atoll(argv[3]);                 /* Virtual address  */

    long long S_bytes = S_MB * (1LL << 20);            /* VAS in bytes     */
    long long P_bytes = P_KB * (1LL << 10);            /* Page size bytes  */

    /* ---------- 2. Total pages ---------- */
    long long N = S_bytes / P_bytes;                   /* # of pages       */

    /* ---------- 3. Validity of virtual address ---------- */
    if (VA < 0 || VA >= S_bytes) {
        printf("Invalid virtual address: out of range [0, %lld)\n", S_bytes);
        return 1;
    }

    /* ---------- 4. Extract page number and offset ---------- */
    long long p = VA / P_bytes;                       /* page number      */
    long long d = VA % P_bytes;                       /* offset           */

    /* ---------- 5. Build a simple page table ----------
     * For demonstration we mark every page as "present" and assign
     * frame number f = (p + 1) * 2 . Any page whose computed p >= N
     * OR whose p >= 4 (for the test) is treated as a miss below.
     */
    long long *PT = (long long *)malloc(sizeof(long long) * N);
    if (!PT) { perror("malloc"); return 1; }

    long long populated_entries = N;            /* entire VAS is mapped  */
    for (long long i = 0; i < populated_entries; ++i)
        PT[i] = (i + 1) * 2;                   /* dummy mapping         */

    /* ---------- 6. Page-table miss check ---------- */
    if (p >= populated_entries) {
        printf("Page table miss for page number %lld\n", p);
        free(PT);
        return 1;
    }

    long long f = PT[p];                       /* frame number          */

    /* ---------- 7. Output ---------- */
    printf("Virtual Address    : %lld\n", VA);
    printf("Page Number (p)    : %lld\n", p);
    printf("Offset       (d)   : %lld\n", d);
    printf("Frame Number (f)   : %lld\n", f);
    printf("Physical Address   : <%lld, %lld>\n", f, d);

    free(PT);
    return 0;
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compilation & Execution (Linux/GCC):**
> ```bash
> gcc -Wall -Wextra -O2 paging.c -o paging
> ./paging 8 4 5000
> ```
> Expected output:
> ```
> Virtual Address    : 5000
> Page Number (p)    : 1
> Offset       (d)   : 904
> Frame Number (f)   : 4
> Physical Address   : <4, 904>
> ```

### 3.4 Variation — The "Page Table Miss" Test Driver

To force a page-table miss (as the NB in the KTU question requires), restrict `populated_entries` to a small number, e.g. `4`, and ask for a VA whose page index is $\ge 4$:

```c
long long populated_entries = 4;   /* only first 4 pages mapped   */
...
if (p >= populated_entries) {
    printf("Page table miss for page number %lld\n", p);
    return 1;
}
```

For the same call `./paging 8 4 5000`, the page number is `1 < 4`, so it succeeds.
But `./paging 8 4 100000` gives $p = 24 \ge 4$, hence **"Page table miss"**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start main] --> B[argc == 4?]
    B -- No --> B1[Print usage and exit]
    B -- Yes --> C[Read argv1 argv2 argv3]
    C --> D[Compute S_bytes and P_bytes]
    D --> E[Compute N = S_bytes / P_bytes]
    E --> F[VA within 0 to S_bytes?]
    F -- No --> F1[Print Invalid VA and exit]
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute p = VA / P_bytes]
    G --> H[Compute d = VA mod P_bytes]
    H --> I[Allocate Page Table PT of size N]
    I --> J[Populate PT with frame numbers]
    J --> K{p less than populated_entries?}
    K -- No --> K1[Print Page table miss and exit]
    K -- Yes --> L[Fetch f = PT of p]
    L --> M[Print physical address as f comma d]
    M --> N[Free PT]
    N --> O[End]
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow Diagram of the Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VA[Virtual Address VA] --> DEC[Decode p and d]
    DEC --> P[Page Number p]
    DEC --> D[Offset d]
    P --> PT[Page Table Array]
    PT --> F[Frame Number f]
    F --> RECON[Reconstruct Physical Address]
    D --> RECON
    RECON --> OUT[Output as f comma d]
```

### 4.3 Mapping from Virtual to Physical Address (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VAS[Virtual Address Space]
        direction LR
        P0[Page 0] --> P1[Page 1] --> P2[Page 2] --> Pk[Page k] --> Pn[Page N-1]
    end

    subgraph PT[Page Table]
        direction TB
        PT0[PT0 equals f0] --> PT1[PT1 equals f1] --> PT2[PT2 equals f2] --> PTk[PTk equals fk]
    end

    subgraph RAM[Physical Memory]
        direction LR
        F0[Frame 0] --> F1[Frame 1] --> F2[Frame 2] --> Fk[Frame k] --> Fn[Frame M-1]
    end

    Pk -.maps to.-> PTk
    PTk -.points to.-> Fk
```

### 4.4 Translation Sequence as a Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU / User
    participant SIM as Paging Program
    participant PT as Page Table Array
    participant RAM as Physical Memory

    CPU->>SIM: ./paging S_MB P_KB VA
    SIM->>SIM: Convert S and P to bytes
    SIM->>SIM: Compute p and d
    SIM->>PT: Index PT with p
    alt p within mapped range
        PT-->>SIM: Return frame number f
        SIM-->>CPU: Physical Address equals f comma d
    else p out of range
        PT-->>SIM: No entry
        SIM-->>CPU: Declare Page table miss
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define the following terms with one-line examples: (i) Virtual address, (ii) Page, (iii) Frame, (iv) Page table.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Virtual address:** A logical address generated by the CPU that the process *believes* is its own. *Example: 5000 in a 32-bit address space.*
>
> **Page:** A fixed-size block of the virtual address space. *Example: 4 KB block containing bytes 4096–8191.*
>
> **Frame:** A fixed-size block of physical memory that holds one page. *Example: Frame 5 in RAM.*
>
> **Page table:** A per-process data structure (typically an array) that maps each virtual page number to a physical frame number, e.g. `PT[3] = 7` means virtual page 3 is in frame 7.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*In a paging system with a 16 MB virtual address space and a page size of 4 KB, how many pages does the process have? Compute the page number and offset for the virtual address 25000.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
S_b = 16 \times 2^{20} = 16777216 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
P_b = 4 \times 2^{10} = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
N = \frac{16777216}{4096} = 4096 \text{ pages}
$$

$$
p = \lfloor 25000 \,/\, 4096 \rfloor = \lfloor 6.10 \rfloor = 6
$$

$$
d = 25000 \bmod 4096 = 25000 - 6 \times 4096 = 25000 - 24576 = 424
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 4096, \quad p = 6, \quad d = 424}
$$

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the paging-based address translation mechanism with a neat diagram. Show how a virtual address is split into page number and offset, and how the page table is consulted to obtain the frame number.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Write a C program that accepts three command-line arguments — the size of the virtual address space (in MB), the page size (in KB), and a virtual address (in decimal). The program should output the physical address in `<frame number, offset>` format. Assume the page table is an array indexed by page number and declare a page table miss if the entry is absent.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(Detailed Explanation)*

In a paging system:

- The virtual address space of the process is divided into equally sized **pages**.
- Physical memory is divided into equally sized **frames** of the same size.
- A page table maintained by the OS holds a mapping `virtual page → physical frame`.
- The **MMU** (Memory Management Unit) splits a virtual address $VA$ into a high-order **page number** $p$ and a low-order **offset** $d$:
$$p = \left\lfloor \frac{VA}{P_b} \right\rfloor, \quad d = VA \bmod P_b$$
- The frame number is fetched: $f = PT[p]$.
- The physical address is the pair $\langle f, d \rangle$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VA[Virtual Address] --> SPLIT[Split p and d]
    SPLIT --> P[Page Number p]
    SPLIT --> D[Offset d]
    P --> PT[Page Table]
    PT --> F[Frame Number f]
    F --> COMB[Combine]
    D --> COMB
    COMB --> PA[Physical Address equals f comma d]
```

**(b)** *(Reference C Program)*

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    if (argc != 4) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <VAS_MB> <PageSize_KB> <VirtualAddress>\n", argv[0]);
        return 1;
    }

    long long S_MB   = atoll(argv[1]);
    long long P_KB   = atoll(argv[2]);
    long long VA     = atoll(argv[3]);

    long long S_bytes = S_MB * (1LL << 20);
    long long P_bytes = P_KB * (1LL << 10);
    long long N       = S_bytes / P_bytes;

    if (VA < 0 || VA >= S_bytes) {
        printf("Invalid virtual address.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    long long p = VA / P_bytes;
    long long d = VA % P_bytes;

    /* For demonstration, treat the first 4 pages as the only mapped ones */
    long long populated = 4;
    if (p >= populated) {
        printf("Page table miss for page number %lld\n", p);
        return 1;
    }

    long long PT[] = {5, 9, 1, 7};   /* arbitrary frame numbers */
    long long f = PT[p];

    printf("Physical address: <%lld, %lld>\n", f, d);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample run:**
```
$ ./paging 8 4 5000
Physical address: <9, 904>
```

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* A system has a 4 MB virtual address space with a 2 KB page size. For the virtual address `12345`, compute: (i) total number of pages, (ii) page number, (iii) offset.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Modify the program from Question A to **also** validate the input: it should reject virtual addresses that are negative or exceed the virtual address space. Show sample runs for two valid and one invalid case.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

$$
P_b = 2 \times 2^{10} = 2048 \text{ bytes}, \quad S_b = 4 \times 2^{20} = 4194304 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
N = \frac{4194304}{2048} = 2048 \text{ pages}
$$

$$
p = \left\lfloor \frac{12345}{2048} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 6.03 \rfloor = 6
$$

$$
d = 12345 \bmod 2048 = 12345 - 6 \times 2048 = 12345 - 12288 = 57
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 2048, \quad p = 6, \quad d = 57}
$$

**(b)** *(Modified Program with Input Validation)*

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    if (argc != 4) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <VAS_MB> <PageSize_KB> <VirtualAddress>\n", argv[0]);
        return 1;
    }

    long long S_MB   = atoll(argv[1]);
    long long P_KB   = atoll(argv[2]);
    long long VA     = atoll(argv[3]);

    if (S_MB <= 0 || P_KB <= 0) {
        printf("Invalid memory parameters.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    long long S_bytes = S_MB * (1LL << 20);
    long long P_bytes = P_KB * (1LL << 10);
    long long N       = S_bytes / P_bytes;

    /* ---- Input validation ---- */
    if (VA < 0) {
        printf("Invalid virtual address: negative value.\n");
        return 1;
    }
    if (VA >= S_bytes) {
        printf("Invalid virtual address: exceeds VAS of %lld bytes.\n", S_bytes);
        return 1;
    }

    long long p = VA / P_bytes;
    long long d = VA % P_bytes;

    long long populated = 4;
    if (p >= populated) {
        printf("Page table miss for page number %lld\n", p);
        return 1;
    }

    long long PT[] = {5, 9, 1, 7};
    long long f = PT[p];

    printf("Physical address: <%lld, %lld>\n", f, d);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample runs:**
```
$ ./paging 8 4 100
Physical address: <5, 100>

$ ./paging 8 4 5000
Physical address: <9, 904>

$ ./paging 8 4 999999999
Invalid virtual address: exceeds VAS of 8388608 bytes.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Wrong page-count formula** — students often write $N = S/P$ without unit normalization. Always convert MB→bytes and KB→bytes **first**.
> 2. **Modular arithmetic confusion** — using `%` with negative numbers in C yields negative offsets; ensure `VA >= 0` *before* computing $d$.
> 3. **Integer overflow** — `int` is 32-bit; with MB-sized spaces the multiplication can overflow. Use `long long` (`int64_t`).
> 4. **Page table miss vs. invalid VA** — distinguish clearly: an out-of-range $VA$ is *invalid*; a valid $VA$ whose page is unmapped is a *page table miss*.
> 5. **Output format** — the KTU problem statement demands `<frame, offset>`. Don't print a single integer; print the pair in angle brackets with a comma.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inputs:** `argv[1]` = VAS in MB, `argv[2]` = page size in KB, `argv[3]` = virtual address in decimal.
- **Normalize units first:** $S_b = S \times 2^{20}$, $P_b = P \times 2^{10}$.
- **Number of pages:** $N = S_b / P_b$.
- **Page number:** $p = VA / P_b$ (integer division).
- **Offset:** $d = VA \bmod P_b$.
- **Frame number:** $f = PT[p]$.
- **Two distinct error cases:**
  - *Invalid VA* — $VA$ is negative or $\ge S_b$.
  - *Page table miss* — $p$ is out of the populated range.
- **Always use `long long`** for byte calculations to avoid 32-bit overflow.
- **Output format** is exactly `<f, d>` with angle brackets and a comma — not `(f, d)` or `f:d`.
- **MMU in real hardware** uses bit-shifts when $P_b$ is a power of two, but in C simulation we use division and modulo for generality.
- **Internal fragmentation** is the only form of fragmentation in paging; the offset $d$ is always $< P_b$.
- **External fragmentation is eliminated** because any free frame can hold any page.

> **One-line memory trick for exams:** *"Pages in books, frames on shelves; the index in the register tells you which shelf the box is on."* 📚

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Demand Paging & Page Replacement — Formal Definition

In a system using **demand paging**, pages are loaded into the physical memory (RAM) only when they are actually referenced (demanded) by a running process, not in advance. When the process requests a page that is **not present** in any of the allocated frames, a hardware trap called a **page fault** is generated. The OS then:

1. Suspends the process.
2. Consults the **page table** to confirm the page is valid but not in memory.
3. Selects a **free frame** (if one exists) OR invokes a **page replacement algorithm** to evict a victim page.
4. Issues disk I/O to bring the demanded page into the chosen frame.
5. Updates the page table and resumes the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Page Fault:** A hardware-generated exception (trap) that occurs when a process accesses a page that is **not currently resident** in any of its allocated physical frames. The OS service routine handles the fault by loading the required page from secondary storage.

A **page-reference string** is a sequence of page numbers that a process accesses over a period of time. For example:
$$\text{Reference String} = 7, 0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 0, 4, 2, 3, 0, 3, 2, 1, 2, 0, 1$$

Page-replacement algorithms decide **which existing page to evict** when a fresh page must be loaded and no free frame is available. The two algorithms covered in this experiment are **FIFO** and **Optimal**.

---

## 1.2 Algorithm 1 — FIFO (First-In, First-Out)

**FIFO** is the simplest page-replacement algorithm. It associates each page with the time at which it was brought into memory. When a page must be replaced, the **oldest page** (the one that has been in memory the longest) is chosen as the victim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIFO Definition (Silberschatz / Galvin / Gagne — OS Concepts):**
> "When a page must be replaced, the oldest page is chosen — the page that has been in memory the longest."

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a queue of people waiting at a ticket counter. The person at the **front of the queue (oldest arrival)** is served (replaced) first. The just-arrived requester joins at the **rear**. This is exactly how FIFO works with frames — the **oldest loaded page** is evicted to make room for the newly referenced page.

---

## 1.3 Algorithm 2 — Optimal Page Replacement (OPT / Belady's Optimal)

The **Optimal algorithm** (also known as **Belady's Algorithm** or **OPT**) is a theoretical benchmark. It replaces the page that will **not be used for the longest period in the future**. By doing so, it guarantees the **lowest possible page-fault rate** for a given reference string and a given number of frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Definition:**
> "Replace the page that will not be used for the longest period of time in the future."

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a librarian who has a limited number of shelves (frames) and a list of books that will be requested in the future (future reference string). A clairvoyant librarian always removes the book that **won't be needed for the longest time**, keeping the shelf space for books that will be demanded soon. This yields the *minimum* number of replacements — but in reality, the OS cannot see the future, so OPT is used only as a **theoretical yardstick** to measure the performance of practical algorithms.

---

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Syllabus Mapping** | Maps directly to **Module 2 (Process & Memory Management)** of the OS theory course — virtual memory & page replacement. |
| **CO Mapping** | CO₂ — *Apply process synchronization and memory management techniques.* |
| **PO Mapping** | PO1 (Engineering Knowledge), PO5 (Modern Tool Usage — `rand()` / `srand()`). |
| **Skill Outcome** | Translate an algorithm from theory into working C code using arrays & command-line arguments. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Demand Paging Assumption:** This experiment assumes **demand paging**, i.e., the very first reference to a page always triggers a page fault (cold start) because the frame is initially empty. Successive references to a page already in memory do not fault.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of FIFO

The FIFO algorithm can be implemented using two data structures:
- A circular **queue** of frame indices (to know which frame holds the oldest page).
- A **set / linear search** of the current frames (to detect hits and misses).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Maintain a **pointer (head index)** that points to the oldest loaded frame.
2. For each page in the reference string:
   - **Step A (Hit Check):** Scan all frames. If the page is already present → **page hit**, increment `hits`, do nothing.
   - **Step B (Miss → Fault):** Otherwise, this is a **page fault**, increment `faults`.
     - If a **free frame** is available, load the page into it.
     - If **no free frame** is available, replace the page at position `head` with the new page, then advance `head` circularly: `head = (head + 1) % num_frames`.
3. After processing the entire string, `faults` is the final answer.

### FIFO — Pseudocode Skeleton

```
faults = 0
frames = [-1] * num_frames
head   = 0
for each page p in ref_string:
    if p not in frames:
        faults += 1
        frames[head] = p
        head = (head + 1) % num_frames
```

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Optimal (Belady's)

The Optimal algorithm requires a **look-ahead** of the future reference string.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. For each page `p` currently requested at position `i` in the reference string:
   - **Hit Check:** If `p` is already in a frame → page hit, move on.
   - **Miss (Page Fault):**
     - If a free frame is available → place `p` there.
     - Else, for **every** page currently in a frame, scan **forward** in the reference string (from `i+1` onwards) to find the **next occurrence index** of that page.
     - Choose the page whose **next-use index is the largest** (i.e., used farthest in the future — or never used again, in which case the next-use index is `+∞`).
     - Replace that page with `p`.

### Optimal — Pseudocode Skeleton

```
faults = 0
for i in 0 .. n-1:
    p = ref[i]
    if p not in frames:
        faults += 1
        if a free frame exists:
            place p in free frame
        else:
            victim = frame whose page has the largest next-use index
                    (∞ if never used again)
            replace victim's page with p
```

> [!TIP]
> **Tie-Breaking Rule:** If two pages in memory are both **never used again** in the future, any of them may be chosen as the victim. This is implementation-defined; in the reference C program below, the **first such page** encountered during scanning is evicted.

---

## 2.3 Belady's Anomaly — Important Theoretical Note

> [!WARNING]
> **Belady's Anomaly (a favourite KTU viva question):**
> For FIFO, it is *not* always true that more frames ⇒ fewer faults. In some reference strings, increasing the number of frames can actually **increase** the number of page faults. This counter-intuitive behaviour is called **Belady's Anomaly**. Optimal and LRU are **stack algorithms** and are immune to it.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Page-fault rate | $P_f$ | $P_f = \dfrac{\text{Number of page faults}}{\text{Length of reference string}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| Page-hit rate | $P_h$ | $P_h = 1 - P_f$ | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| Reference string length | $n$ | Number of page references in the string | Integer |
| Page number range | $r$ | $\text{page} \in \{0, 1, \dots, 9\}$ (in this experiment) | Integer |
| Number of frames | $f$ | $1 \le f \le 7$ (received via command line) | Integer |
| FIFO head advance | $h$ | $h = (h + 1) \bmod f$ | Modular index |
| OPT next-use index | $\nu(p)$ | Position of next occurrence of page $p$ in future; $\infty$ if never | Index / ∞ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundaries to remember:** $0 \le \text{page number} \le 9$, $1 \le f \le 7$, $n \ge 1$. Pages are 0-indexed as in the reference string.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Buffer Pools:** Real systems approximate OPT using techniques such as **2Q** and **ARC** (Adaptive Replacement Cache) to evict the least-useful blocks.
- **Web Browser Caching:** Eviction policies like LRU approximate optimal caching for tab/page caching.
- **CPU Cache Replacement:** Modern Intel/AMD caches use **pseudo-LRU** (tree-based PLRU) which are descendants of the optimal ideal.
- **OS Kernel Reclaim:** The Linux kernel's page reclaim logic is closer to a working-set / clock algorithm — both inspired by the OPT ideal.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example — FIFO Trace

**Given:**
- Reference String (length $n = 12$): $\;7,\,0,\,1,\,2,\,0,\,3,\,0,\,4,\,2,\,3,\,0,\,3$
- Number of frames: $f = 3$
- Initial state: all frames empty.

**Step-by-step trace (FIFO):**

| Step | Page | Frames (left = oldest) | Fault? | Fault Count | Action |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | 7 | [7, -, -] | ✅ Fault | 1 | Load 7 at head=0; head→1 |
| 2 | 0 | [7, 0, -] | ✅ Fault | 2 | Load 0 at head=1; head→2 |
| 3 | 1 | [7, 0, 1] | ✅ Fault | 3 | Load 1 at head=2; head→0 |
| 4 | 2 | [2, 0, 1] | ✅ Fault | 4 | Replace head=0 (page 7) with 2; head→1 |
| 5 | 0 | [2, 0, 1] | ❌ Hit | 4 | 0 already in frame 1 |
| 6 | 3 | [2, 3, 1] | ✅ Fault | 5 | Replace head=1 (page 0) with 3; head→2 |
| 7 | 0 | [2, 3, 0] | ✅ Fault | 6 | Replace head=2 (page 1) with 0; head→0 |
| 8 | 4 | [4, 3, 0] | ✅ Fault | 7 | Replace head=0 (page 2) with 4; head→1 |
| 9 | 2 | [4, 2, 0] | ✅ Fault | 8 | Replace head=1 (page 3) with 2; head→2 |
| 10 | 3 | [4, 2, 3] | ✅ Fault | 9 | Replace head=2 (page 0) with 3; head→0 |
| 11 | 0 | [0, 2, 3] | ✅ Fault | 10 | Replace head=0 (page 4) with 0; head→1 |
| 12 | 3 | [0, 2, 3] | ❌ Hit | 10 | 3 already in frame 2 |

$$\text{Total Page Faults (FIFO)} = 10$$

$$\text{Page-Fault Rate} = P_f = \frac{10}{12} = 0.8333$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example — OPT Trace (Same String)

For OPT we look *forward* to decide which page to evict.

| Step | Page | Frames | Fault? | Faults | Reasoning (which page to evict, if needed) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | 7 | [7, -, -] | ✅ | 1 | Empty frame available |
| 2 | 0 | [7, 0, -] | ✅ | 2 | Empty frame available |
| 3 | 1 | [7, 0, 1] | ✅ | 3 | Empty frame available |
| 4 | 2 | [2, 0, 1] | ✅ | 4 | Frames contain {7,0,1}. Future uses: 7→never, 0→at step 5, 1→never. Evict **7** (or 1, both never used). Evict 7. |
| 5 | 0 | [2, 0, 1] | ❌ | 4 | Hit |
| 6 | 3 | [2, 0, 3] | ✅ | 5 | Future uses from step 6: 2→at step 9, 0→at step 7, 1→never. Evict **1** (never used). |
| 7 | 0 | [2, 0, 3] | ❌ | 5 | Hit |
| 8 | 4 | [4, 0, 3] | ✅ | 6 | Future uses: 2→at step 9, 0→at step 11, 3→at step 10. **All three used.** Evict page whose next use is **farthest** = 0 (at step 11). Wait — distances: 2→step 9 (Δ=1), 0→step 11 (Δ=3), 3→step 10 (Δ=2). Farthest is **0** at step 11. Evict 0. |
| 9 | 2 | [4, 2, 3] | ✅ | 7 | Future uses from step 9: 4→never, 2→never, 3→step 10. Evict the one with farthest use: **4** (never) or **2** (never). Evict 4. |
| 10 | 3 | [4, 2, 3] | ❌ | 7 | Hit |
| 11 | 0 | [0, 2, 3] | ✅ | 8 | Future uses from step 11: 0→never, 2→step 12, 3→step 12. Evict farthest = **0** (never). |
| 12 | 3 | [0, 2, 3] | ❌ | 8 | Hit |

$$\text{Total Page Faults (OPT)} = 8$$

$$\text{Page-Fault Rate (OPT)} = P_f = \frac{8}{12} = 0.6667$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** For the same reference string and frame count, OPT produced **fewer** faults than FIFO (8 < 10). This is precisely why OPT is treated as the theoretical lower bound — no other algorithm can do better on the same string.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Expression of the OPT Cost Function

For a given reference string $R = (r_0, r_1, \dots, r_{n-1})$ and $f$ frames, let $\nu(p, i)$ denote the **next-use index** of page $p$ *after* position $i$:

$$
\nu(p, i) =
\begin{cases}
k, & \text{if } r_k = p \text{ for some } k > i, \text{ and } k = \min\{j > i : r_j = p\} \\
\infty, & \text{if } p \notin \{r_{i+1}, r_{i+2}, \dots, r_{n-1}\}
\end{cases}
$$

At any page fault at position $i$, OPT evicts the page $p^*$ satisfying:

$$
p^* = \underset{p \,\in\, \text{frames at time } i}{\arg\max}\;\nu(p, i)
$$

The total page-fault count is:

$$
F_{opt}(R, f) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \mathbf{1}_{\{\,r_i \,\notin\, \text{frames at time } i\,\}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In simple words: **OPT always evicts the page whose next-use index $\nu$ is the largest** (equivalently, the page used farthest in the future, with "never used again" treated as $\infty$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | FIFO | OPT |
|---|---|---|
| Future knowledge required? | No | **Yes** (full reference string) |
| Practical implementable? | **Yes** (queue) | No (only theoretical) |
| Optimal in page-fault count? | No | **Yes (lower bound)** |
| Suffers from Belady's Anomaly? | **Yes** | No |
| Time complexity (per reference) | $O(f)$ linear search | $O(f \cdot n)$ naive, $O(n)$ with precomputation |
| Space complexity | $O(f)$ | $O(f + n)$ (needs future string) |

### Derivation of the Hit-Miss Indicator (Hit Test)

For any reference position $i$ and frame set $\mathcal{F}_i$ at time $i$:

$$
\text{Hit}(i) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } r_i \in \mathcal{F}_i \\
0, & \text{otherwise (page fault)}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Total Page Faults} \; F = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\bigl(1 - \text{Hit}(i)\bigr) = n - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\text{Hit}(i)
$$

**Numerical verification** for FIFO on our 12-reference string:
$$F_{FIFO} = n - \text{hits} = 12 - 2 = 10 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Numerical verification** for OPT on our 12-reference string:
$$F_{OPT} = 12 - 4 = 8 \;\;\checkmark$$

flowchart TD

    S1 --> S2{i less than n?}

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Optimal Page Replacement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O0([Start OPT function]) --> O1[Initialize frames with -1 and faults = 0 and frameCount = 0]
    O1 --> O2{i less than n?}
    O2 -- No --> O3([Return faults])
    O2 -- Yes --> O4[page = ref i]
    O4 --> O5{page in frames?}
    O5 -- Yes --> O6[i = i + 1 then back to O2]
    O5 -- No --> O7[faults = faults + 1]

```

The complete program is composed of four cooperating modules. The interactions are mapped below:

flowchart LR

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Define page fault. Explain how demand paging differs from anticipatory paging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Page Fault (1 mark):** A hardware-generated trap that occurs when a process accesses a page which is not currently present in any of the allocated physical frames.
- **Demand Paging (1 mark):** Pages are brought into memory *only when* they are first referenced by the process. No pages are pre-loaded.
- **Anticipatory Paging (1 mark):** The OS tries to predict which pages a process will need soon (e.g., by prefetching sequential pages or based on heuristics) and loads them in advance, even before a page fault occurs. Demand paging waits for the fault; anticipatory paging preempts it.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
**Why is the Optimal page replacement algorithm not used in real systems, even though it gives the lowest fault rate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reason 1 (1.5 marks):** OPT requires complete *future knowledge* of the reference string — the OS must know which pages will be referenced and when. The OS has no mechanism to predict the future accurately.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** The cost of looking ahead through arbitrarily long reference strings is too high.
- **Reason 3 (0.5 mark):** OPT is therefore used only as a **theoretical benchmark** to evaluate practical algorithms like FIFO, LRU, and Clock.

---
